-   [2015-06-11 - Genova GI Effects Comprehensive Stool Profile](https://www.dropbox.com/s/y0c2ti3dleumkhx/2015-06-11%20-%20Genova%20-%20GI%20Effects%20Comp%20Profile%20%232200%20Report_.pdf?dl=0) - Found very low diversity of gut bacteria, high pancreatic Elastase, high fecal fat
-   [2015-06-15 - Wellness FX Blood Testing](https://www.dropbox.com/s/6fg7mdvq3ryn6m8/2015-06-15%20-%20Wellness%20FX_.pdf?dl=0) - High Omega 3's, High Reverse T3, High FSH
-   [2015-09-22 - HIDA Scan](https://www.dropbox.com/s/vdf6mq02eir3psu/2015-09-22%20-%20HIDA%20Scan_.pdf?dl=0) - Cystic Duct Syndrome of the Gallbladder
-   [2015-09-25 - Bone Density Scan](https://www.dropbox.com/s/ww1czko649fxmis/2015-09-25%20-%20Bone%20Density_.pdf?dl=0)
-   [2015-09-25 - WellnessFX + imported labs (list)](https://www.dropbox.com/s/128ndzyblm8koj0/2015-09-25%20-%20WellnessFX%20%2B%20imported%20labs%20-%20List_.pdf?dl=0)
-   [2015-09-25 - WellnessFX + imported labs (sparkline chart)](https://www.dropbox.com/s/3ngug1owoa73193/2015-09-25%20-%20WellnessFX%20%2B%20imported%20labs%20-%20Sparkline%20Chart_.pdf?dl=0)
-   [2015-09-26 - MRI of Abdomen Report](https://www.dropbox.com/s/pg958jlcqovagxi/2015-09-26%20-%20MRI%20Scan%20of%20Whole%20Abdomen%20Report_.pdf?dl=0) ([All Images](https://www.dropbox.com/s/pg958jlcqovagxi/2015-09-26%20-%20MRI%20Scan%20of%20Whole%20Abdomen%20Report_.pdf?dl=0)), [Youtube Videos](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/rubicons-labs.17326/#post-207556) - Found mild enlargement of liver
-   [2015-12-31 - Blood Tests @ Annual Physical](https://www.dropbox.com/s/7v8hezoea7dpn0g/2015-12-31%20-%20Labs%20%28Annual%20Physical%29_.pdf?dl=0)
-   2016-01-12 - Ubiome ([Bacteria](https://www.dropbox.com/s/dttcf8snsvju2rp/2016-01-12%20-%20Ubiome%20Bacteria_.pdf?dl=0)) ([Function](https://www.dropbox.com/s/ft1wntrh2ucw3lq/2016-01-12%20-%20Ubiome%20Functions_.pdf?dl=0)) - Gut bacteria diversity continues to fall

-   [2016-02-06 - Telomere from Spectracell](https://www.dropbox.com/s/5l0h868xs2vn5e3/2016-02-06%20-%20Spectracell%20Telomere_.pdf?dl=0) - Telomere length of a 90 year old
-   [2016-02-08 - Vitamin D from Rocky Mountain Analytical](https://www.dropbox.com/s/1c4rir6isq3chle/2016-02-08%20-%20VitaminD%20from%20Rocky%20Mountain%20Analytical_.pdf?dl=0) Optimal Vitamin D @ 58 ng/mL
-   [2016-02-28 - Saliva Cortisol](https://www.dropbox.com/s/07dcwrwz4bjfjs3/2016-02-28%20-%20Saliva%20Cortisol_.pdf?dl=0) - Very low from morning to evening

-   2016-08-24 - [Optometrist visit](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/rubicons-labs.17326/#post-211089) - Slightly improved vision, lowered intraocular pressure
-   2016-09-23 - [Vitamin D](https://www.dropbox.com/s/jaw1sfodlk42tlo/2016-09-23%20-%20Vitamin%20D%20from%20Rocky%20Mountain%20Analytical_.pdf?dl=0) - Low normal

### Rubicon — 2016-01-17T04:43:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rubicons-labs.17326/post-186551>

Dropbox link : [Blood Tests @ Annual Physical](https://www.dropbox.com/s/i1558y9fd2wnx3v/2015-12-31%20-%20Labs%20%28Annual%20Physical%29_.pdf?dl=0) Dec 2015

Highlights:
ALT is high again 66.5 (Ref <60 U/L)
Iron is high 33 (Ref 8-30 umol/L)
Iron Saturation is high 0.62 (Ref 0.15-0.50)
Platelet count is way down again 67 (Ref 160-400 x10\*9/L)

I'm almost thinking I should lay off of all the seafood & protein to try and lower my iron. Fat doesn't really digest, and the lack of carbs seems to be a disadvantage.

Liver is really sore these days. I feel like my heart is beating 'hard'... for example when I lay down in bed I can noticeably feel my heart beating. This is in comparison to a 'normal' time where I can't feel it at all. Perhaps something to do with platelet count.

### Rubicon — 2016-03-03T05:52:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rubicons-labs.17326/post-190118>

Dropbox link: Ubiome ([Bacteria](https://www.dropbox.com/s/dttcf8snsvju2rp/2016-01-12%20-%20Ubiome%20Bacteria_.pdf?dl=0)) ([Function](https://www.dropbox.com/s/ft1wntrh2ucw3lq/2016-01-12%20-%20Ubiome%20Functions_.pdf?dl=0)) January 2016

Unfortunately, in this test my gut bacteria seems to have simplified further in some ways since the last time I tested 10 months ago in March of 2015.

I did this Ubiome measurement shortly before going for a sun filled vacation in which I observed significant changes in my stool consistency. I plan to follow this up with another test soon...it seems my abdominal issues have calmed down since going on vacation and subsequently beginning a course of Betaine HCL.

### lilreddgirl — 2016-03-03T07:07:45+0200

Also me personally I started to eat a lot - 3 oz split in two - of Kelp Granules daily and it's dropped my desire or need for other seafood, but I seem to want more seaweeds now. It seems to help my liver soon as I eat them, maybe they just neutralize stuff in my intestines, maybe theres more to it. But it's really regulated my intestinal movements, I think a lot of the Kelp is just brushing out my intestines and not getting fully digested... kind of like insoluble fiber but with iodine and stuff mixed in to neutralize toxins  It's hard to find a way to eat the kelp granules so I've been mixing with Kefir or Yoghurt, but you can try any other kind of oily/paste stuff for the texture...

I haven't tried liver/gallbladder flushes. Considered it, but whenever I researched them they alway seemed to be woo.

### lilreddgirl — 2016-03-03T20:02:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rubicons-labs.17326/post-190143>

> [Rubicon said:](/goto/post?id=190132)
>
> At least I have a baseline to work from now. I'd rather know than not.
> It seems [I'm not the only person on this forum](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-un-official-telomere-before-and-after-thread.518/page-4#post-94166) that has a telomere age over 80. More than ever, I'm sure I need to find & fix whatever it is that's the problem.
>
>
> Reduce protein and replace with carbs? Since starting the HCL I'm feeling like I might actually be digesting fats and my gallbladder pain has almost been non-existent.
>
> I haven't tried liver/gallbladder flushes. Considered it, but whenever I researched them they alway seemed to be woo.

Reduce protein and don't replace with starchy carbs but rather non starchies like, just greens like salad, kales, spinach etc and onions and bell peppers maybe olives and avocado and other non starchy veggies. When it gets hot in your area try adding fruits.

The liver/gallbladder flushes are definitely not woo, though stuff considered woo can work too. Not sure in what way you were thinking of it that way, though maybe it's because I've seen them recommended by mostly alternative or naturopathic practitioners online.

Flushes of different kinds like this were recommended by medical doctors (in other countries) often until recently, and probably still are in some places. I think often times these were not described as 'flushes' though, but that's what was happening... stones being passed in response to some kind of prescription, whether drinking certain minerals in water, plant oils, or something like kilograms of crushed apples.

They're very body/physical and have strong body/physical effects. I've done it a couple times before and experienced very physical effects, ie passing stones... but stopped short as one needs to do a series to really get the effect going. (In the first flushes beginning less stones will be passed than will in later flushes).

After these first two I experienced a similar stone flushing event from eating raw oysters (I think bacteria on oysters triggered lots of bile release for me, and my billiary tubing was more open due to the flushes, as other oyster incidents since caused indigestion but not stone flushing), I also realized before I found Dr. K I experienced something similar from eating a grapefruit on a day I hadn't eaten anything... so I figured that I will stop flushes and just focus on keto epi-paleo diet and see if flushing would occur on it's own. The sweet tea I was consuming pre flush also did not agree with my tummy during winter and I figured it was bringing me out of keto at the wrong time. Winter is not a recommended time for flushes anyway as I understand cold (foods, liquid, and exposure) tends to temporarily constrict liver biliary tubing.

Well as far as I know American/Canadian/Western medicine doesn't have much to help this underactive gallbladder, liver 'tension' situation which is very common I think. Even if one were to have calcified gallstones that can be seen with ultra sound (rather than cholesterol soft stones that just reduce gallbladder function and liver function), the rx is gallbladder removal which is no bueno.

I myself think that triggering some flushes now in spring is one of the most straightforward ways to help my liver. The practice I'm thinking of is pretty much standard as I see online, involves magnesium, grapefruits and olive oils, and maybe some apple juice, so nothing that would not be part of natural human consumption. There's lots of info actually a huge a amount of info I found online of people who have triggered this biliary flushing and had results. You mentioned reduced gallbladder output and I think if I'm not confusing you with someone else, you mentioned awhile ago having pains or issues predominantly on the right side of your body, this is similar to me and I think could definitely relate to liver biliary tube 'clogging.'

It's possible that eating Epi-paleo but with HIGH seasonal carbs in the summer of foods that can reduce stones (like lecithin, certain oils, or tons of apples or cranberry juice or black cherry juice), can improve the condition - ie melt and eliminate cholesterol stones without triggering a direct flush. One would probably also need to have a really good magnesium status to have biliary tubes relaxed enough for things to flow well. Probably the magnesium status is a big key to reducing fatty liver disease the epi paleo way. I remember reading the blog from a few years back of how Dr. K had suggested to I think it was Ben Greenfield ways to heal his pre-diabetes, and taking a bunch of epsom salt baths, and magnesium chloride foot soaks and even I think oral magnesium was a big part of that, along with CT and epi-paleo.

CT I would think in a way works against this process by constricting tubules, but if one were to get cold adapted well perhaps the mitochondria would learn to generate their own heat therefore eventually creating a warmer, more relaxed environment that did not constrict as strongly in response cold. This may take some time though and I think magnesium level def has to be addressed. I'm wondering if using Epsom Salt in CT baths might help in this respect, as one absorbs magnesium even from cold ocean water...

### Rubicon — 2016-06-17T05:07:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rubicons-labs.17326/post-196742>

I wanted to confirm the Quicksilver Heavy Metals blood test from April with a hair test. The hair test found that I have high mercury levels and the test included a fair bit of interpretation: Dropbox Link: [Rocky Mountain Analytical Hair Element Analysis](https://www.dropbox.com/s/7d16f8rqzmrbcln/2016-05-18%20-%20Rocky%20Mountain%20Analytical%20Hair%20Element%20Anaylsis_.pdf?dl=0)

Mercury: 1.7ug/g Reference range: <1.2ug/g. This level is in the ~96 percentile of exposure levels and is considered High (red bar)

From the interpretation:

MERCURY HIGHER THAN AVERAGE OR HIGH (YELLOW OR RED BAR)
Mercury is above average (yellow bar) or high (red bar). Sources of mercury exposure include seafood (especially larger fish toward or at the top of the food chain), vaccines received prior to around 2002, allergy shots (received from the 1960's through 1990's), silvery coloured dental amalgams as well as high fructose corn syrup (HFCS). The levels of mercury found in HFCS-containing products are substantially lower than those found in seafood, but children consume large (and increasing) amounts of HCFS, and children are more sensitive to the effects of mercury than adults. In any event, all individuals have exposure to mercury, regardless of age. The principal idea behind RMA's approach (patterned after Dr. Cutler's work) is that mercury can disrupt the hair deposition of elements (including itself). If the essential elements exhibit a normal pattern, the implication is that the measured mercury level accurately reflects body burden, and the patient is tolerating the current burden of mercury (e.g. due to genetics, supplementation, detoxification strategies) even though the result is above the 68th percentile. The best available evidence points to 1 mcg/g as a significant benchmark for children. Below 1 mcg/g, there is little indication of any measureable neurologic impairment in children. Bear in mind however, that if the clinical picture is still suggestive of a problem with mercury, this must take precedence over laboratory testing.

High morning melatonin levels are often present in individuals with Seasonal Affective Disorder. This may be due to prolonged nocturnal production of melatonin, and/or late onset of its production.
This profile reveals a **disturbance in the circadian rhythm** of melatonin. This may influence other hormones such as thyroid, testosterone, and estrogen. As well as playing a crucial role in sleep-wake cycles, melatonin influences other vital functions including cardiovascular and antioxidant protection, endocrine function, immune regulation and body temperature.

### Rubicon — 2017-03-25T18:54:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rubicons-labs.17326/post-211086>

Hi JanSz, thanks very much for the detailed run through. I really appreciate your taking the time to do that.

Regarding low cortisol, high melatonin -- Cortisol test was run in winter 2016 when I was in rough shape. At the time I was not sleeping much, circadian rhythm in terrible shape, etc. Last summer 2016 I got out into the morning sun every day and as much daytime sun as I could. I felt like I really established a good rhythm at that time, and I suspect that has caused my cortisol to improve. I will measure cortisol again this summer 2017.
The very high melatonin test at all times of the day has confounded me. I will re-test this in summer 2017 as well. When I get morning sun + a good amount of daytime sun + block blue light in the evening, I get hit with a wave of massive wave of tiredness around 9:30PM and sleep is awesome.

Regarding iodine, previously I've been using a few drops of Lugols topically on my skin a few days a week. As per your recommendation I'll try increasing the dose more.

I've suspected elevated mercury due to having several amalgam fillings extracted in Dec 2012, and being so sick that my body didn't have the ability to get the mercury out. To address this, I've dabbled with chelation using DMSA/ALA, but lately I've just been focusing on increasing my redox, using full spectrum sauna, and sleeping on a magnetico. I eat a few brazil nuts every day and occasionally supplement with selenium. I plan to repeat a hair mineral analysis to see if I am making any progress with eliminating mercury.

D3 My latest D3 test in Sept 2016 was taken after a summer of sun exposure and no supplementation. I'm going to try repeating this summer and see if things have improved.
This summer I am devising a work area that I can setup to allow me to work on a computer outdoors in the light for several hours per day.
Also, my wife and I are seriously working on moving to Florida. This may be my last Canadian winter. Worst case scenario I hope to only spend a portion of next winter in Canada.

Cholesterol has come up enough in my latest test. I've been consuming large amounts of [grass fed butter](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/making-a-kerrygold-butter-run-canadian-style.17862/) this year. My family doctor has now become concerned. Lol. I'll post those labs next.

EPA/DHA test was taken at a time when I was taking krill oil pills. I stopped taking those after learning about the imbalance in my levels, but I've continued eating oysters/sardines/salmon regularly. I will re-test

Spectracell Micronutrient Analysis - I've been wanting to do this. Will add it to the list.

High Reverse T3 - I have not been supplementing with T4.

Labcorp tests might be a longshot. I can only access Canadian testing without having to pay out of pocket. It seems all the tests you listed would be out of pocket for me.

DHEA - I have not considered taking supplemental DHEA. I'll look into that.

Progesterone, Anastrozole, etc ... I was hoping by minding artificial light, getting more sunlight that I could avoid trying to supplement things like that.

### James Lech — 2019-09-10T08:51:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rubicons-labs.17326/post-277540>

> [Rubicon said:](/goto/post?id=196742)
>
> I wanted to confirm the Quicksilver Heavy Metals blood test from April with a hair test. The hair test found that I have high mercury levels and the test included a fair bit of interpretation: Dropbox Link: [Rocky Mountain Analytical Hair Element Analysis](https://www.dropbox.com/s/7d16f8rqzmrbcln/2016-05-18%20-%20Rocky%20Mountain%20Analytical%20Hair%20Element%20Anaylsis_.pdf?dl=0)
>
> Mercury: 1.7ug/g Reference range: <1.2ug/g. This level is in the ~96 percentile of exposure levels and is considered High (red bar)
>
> From the interpretation:
>
> MERCURY HIGHER THAN AVERAGE OR HIGH (YELLOW OR RED BAR)
> Mercury is above average (yellow bar) or high (red bar). Sources of mercury exposure include seafood (especially larger fish toward or at the top of the food chain), vaccines received prior to around 2002, allergy shots (received from the 1960's through 1990's), silvery coloured dental amalgams as well as high fructose corn syrup (HFCS). The levels of mercury found in HFCS-containing products are substantially lower than those found in seafood, but children consume large (and increasing) amounts of HCFS, and children are more sensitive to the effects of mercury than adults. In any event, all individuals have exposure to mercury, regardless of age. The principal idea behind RMA's approach (patterned after Dr. Cutler's work) is that mercury can disrupt the hair deposition of elements (including itself). If the essential elements exhibit a normal pattern, the implication is that the measured mercury level accurately reflects body burden, and the patient is tolerating the current burden of mercury (e.g. due to genetics, supplementation, detoxification strategies) even though the result is above the 68th percentile. The best available evidence points to 1 mcg/g as a significant benchmark for children. Below 1 mcg/g, there is little indication of any measureable neurologic impairment in children. Bear in mind however, that if the clinical picture is still suggestive of a problem with mercury, this must take precedence over laboratory testing.

Good morning,
I was hoping you could elaborate on:
You write HFCS, but then HCFS later with no abbreviation. are they both the same? As I have been trying to understand what HCFS stands for?

Thank you in advance.

# Thread: Gabe's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 46
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gabe — 2016-01-05T22:43:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-185502>

Hi all, I know a lot of you from the Facebook group, i'm Monica's significant other . Here is my Journal:

Currently I have chronic fatigue, I often have either sleep maintenance insomnia or early morning waking insomnia. Im a sugar addict and the two are linked, in my N=1. If I stick to a strict very low carb diet (leptin rx) , then I can control my sugar cravings and push my sleep maintenance insomnia forward to about 5: 30 AM, which is like 2 hours before sunrise this time of the year for me. If I add sugar or starch, after a few days, im up at 3am with hot flashes and uncontrollable sugar cravings for at least two weeks. When I have sleep maintenance insomnia, I usually go to bed at 10: 30, then im up by 2 or 3, and cant get back to sleep until about 7am. Because of this I am currently not working getting pretty desperate for a solution.

Ive been epi paleo for over a year now, strict red light protocol at night, up and outside in the sun in the AM, sleeping on a magnetico for almost a year, wifi off at night, Skypers after dinner, doing facedunks and the ice on the stomach CT most nights, eating at least one meal of seafood a day.

For my history, I think I've been leptin resistant since I was 4 or 5. Recently looking through family pictures this christmas that is when I noticed I adopted my round faced chubby belly kid look that followed me through adult hood. I grew up in Anchorage Alaska eating junk food, breakfast cereal, pizza, candy, canned food and sleeping with a night light because I was scared of the dark. When I was 11 or so I suffered repeated blunt head trauma for about 3 or 4 months while i was in an abusive relationship with my stepmother. About a year after that I noticed I had trouble focusing in school. When I was in highschool I started working late hours in restaurants and would often pull all nighters working on papers. After highschool I dropped out of college twice. I remember feeling "wired and tired" where I didn't feel sleepy at night but I was exhausted in the mornings. I was always working part time jobs in restaurants where I would get done around 11pm on weeknights.

In 2000 I joined the Navy by getting a waiver for being overweight. After 9/11 I was on night shift every 3 or 4 days, my job being to babysit server backups at midnight, and often stand a guard watch for 4 hours in the the middle of the night. Over the next 4 years I was assigned night shifts about half the time whenever we were out to sea, that meant getting very broken sleep during the day. I remember even when I had day shifts, the only way I got through it was by sneaking 20 minute lunchtime power naps. The only time I saw daylight was on my smoke breaks when we were out to sea.

After that I got desk jobs where I worked in windowless rooms for about 10 years. I struggled with weight, exhaustion, extreme morning fatigue, day time fatigue, and being wired at night. I got diagnosed with Sleep apnea in 2007 and have been using a CPAP since. I started with a pressure at 7 but am up to a pressure of 15 now. It helped with my Vagus nerve response immediately ( I would wake up nauseous until about 10am, but within a day of wearing the CPAP I would wake up famished.) However it never helped with daytime fatigue. I switched to Paleo about 3 and a half years ago. Ive been following the Leptin RX pretty strictly for the last year. In April 2014 I left a toxic work environment to become a full time student and try to recover. Ive been kind of waiting for things to turn around since then with no real progress.

Here are some stressors in case I missed any:

-   Smoking for 10 years
-   extremely high carb sugary diet until about 31 or so
-   Worked in windowless rooms for last 14 years
-   Night time shift-work from about 15-26 years old.
-   Sleep apnea undiagnosed until 26 years old.
-   Prescribed ProVigil by sleep Doctor for a few years for daytime sleepiness. Made me really irritable.
-   Exposure to weird environmental crap on a big Naval ship, e.g. degaussing electo-magnets, high frequency radio antenna, rapidly changing time zones, windowless work environments, bright light 24/7, rotating shift work and broken sleep, constantly working around electronics.
-   Family history of sleep apnea and diabetes, heart disease, brother has Hashimotos.

Here's what I currently take:
Metformin 500mg IR 2x daily (this has really been helping me stay asleep combined with a keto diet)
Probiotic daily
L Tryptophan w/ co B6 500mg - 1x at night to help get to sleep

Here's some biomarkers from 9/14/15
HDL - 27.6
LDL - 247.8
Trig - 139
Glucose - 93 (fasting)
HBA1C - 5.4
HS CRP - 0.7
D25 - 35 (3/31/15)

Homocysteine - 12
BUN - 14
Creatine - 0.9
B/C Ratio - 15.7

I'm getting an MRI done later this week as part of a Veterans Neurological Study, I'm really curious to see if there is any noticeable deterioration in the frontal lobes due to chronic stress either from the childhood blunt trauma or the more recent job/life/sleep deprivation stressors.

Thanks in advance for any help, I really want to figure this out and get healthy and working again.

### Gabe — 2016-01-05T22:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-185503>

I forgot to mention, I lived in the Caymans from 7 to 12 years old. I loved it and was outside all the time and eating local fish for dinner, but that is where I started getting 5 or 6 colds throught the year, and persisted through adulthood. It slowed down when I went Paleo, but I havnt had one since I switched to very low carb epi paleo (Leptin RX).

I forgot to mention when I was in the navy I received the following vaccinations:

-   Small Pox
-   Anthrax
-   yearly flu shots (always gave me acute tired and achy symptoms for three days afterwards)

### Gabe — 2016-01-05T22:54:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-185504>

Last quarter I've also been having symptoms of depression. My morale is really high, but I've been having fatigue, insomnia, irritability, and a really severe inability to focus. I've started the 2mg nicotine gum in the am protocol, but its too early to tell if its been helping, since Christmas happened right in the middle of it.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-05T23:50:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-185506>

sounds like a low dopamine state.........Time 6 Thursday should be an eye opener.

### Monica W. — 2016-01-07T20:16:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-185681>

Great journal sweetie. I hope you can get more feedback.

### Gabe — 2016-02-01T23:04:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-187885>

Jack I think you are really on to something with the low dopamine in the Time series. Is there anything else I can be doing besides the usual CT + Leptin protocols, morning sun + avoiding blue light/nnEMF mitigation + dietary DHA? I did the AM nicotine gum for about a month and a half, it woke me up, but then I was sleepy again an hour or two later.

Here's where Im at right now. I get up, very exhausted and have trouble getting out of bed due to exhaustion/fatigue. I go outside to get sun/ground. Then I eat a BAB, then I clean up around the kitchen and then go for a 25 min walk outside. When I come back in im exhausted and usually need to take a nap. Sometimes I dont even make it to the walk, I just get some more sun and go for the nap. Im so tired of being exhausted all the time.

I finally got a dunk tank for outside and Monica and I are starting to do 10 minute dunks outside when the weather is above 60f. I don't feel like the Gut Buster was doing anything, I wore it at night regularly for about 3 months. I kept getting cryoburn on my stomach when I used it last year so this year I put on a compression shirt and a t-shirt and just loaded two packs in the front, wait 20 min (it takes 15 min to get my tummy surface down to 45 degrees) then i move the two packs to the side and I can leave them there for the next hour without any problems.

### Gabe — 2016-02-01T23:29:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-187886>

Any advice for getting help for chronic fatigue syndrome/adrenal fatigue syndrome/low redox/dopamine from the VA? I called the VA crisis line to let them know I had depression (fatigue, insomnia, irritability, trouble concentrating). They were great and set me up with a psychiatrist in the PTSD clinic (I have never been through a tramatic event, just a bunch of night shifts). All the PTSD psychiatrist and the primary care doctor, that I saw today, knew to do with insomnia and fatigue is to treat the anxiety with antidepressants. I explained to them that I wasnt suffering from anxiety (I quit work a year and a half ago, Im taking a break from school right now, I dont have kids, Monica has graciously agreed to cover the bills temporarily.) Basically at the moment I'm a "kept" man, so no anxiety here.

I **really do not** want to go on antidepressants and fuck with my brain chemistry and have to deal with the potential for tolerance withdrawals, and regular withdrawals. I noticed I had a tolerance after taking 25mg Trazadone for 5 days and then stopping after 5 days made me irritable for a week. Maybe since I dont drink coffee or alcohol I just notice my brian chemistry being fucked with more.

At my request I got a referral to see an endocrinologist this Thursday, but it's in the Diabetes clinic (i dont have diabetes, A1C never higher than 5.6, although i have been leptin resistant/prediabetic for the last 4 years and treating it with low carb and I have a family history of diabetes).

Im hoping if no one has any idea about how to fix my chronic daytime fatigue and sleep mainainance/early morning waking insomnia, at least I can get a Metformin refill from the Diabetes clinic. Any advice on what to say, ask for, or point out to them would be appreciated. Im worried that compared to their normal patients Im going to look relatively healthy and they are going to send me on my way with nothing more than a pat on the back.

Today at the primary care physician I have kind of a young doctor, but at one point I could tell she actually started to "work my problem" a little (were waiting on my records to transfer and take some fasting blood work tomorrow), but she had no idea why someone like me would have symptoms like that other than "anxiety". At one point she even marveled at how awake and alert I seemed and that I even seemed more energetic that she was. I tried to explain that was because I planned my entire day around this doctor visit and I would have to go home and take a nap afterwards. Being an introvert with an invisible illness is a constant pain in the ass. (Just yesterday I tried to be honest with a kayaking buddy about how I have really been having a hard time lately. His response? Go for two hour bike rides every day and then have two beers before bed. Its like, ya that worked in my 20's, completely fucks me in my 30's. It's like I have an alien physiology compared to a healthy leptin sensitive person.

Im trying to keep my eyes on the long term. If my 30's and even my 40's are fucked, then maybe I can fix it by my 50's and be one of those active healthy as a horse whitewater canoeists that can paddle circles around me in their 60's and 70's.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-02-02T02:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-187897>

[https://www.jackkruse.com/time-8-psoriasis-and-the-photoelectric-effect/](https://www.jackkruse.com/time-8-psoriasis-and-the-photoelectric-effect/)

### Gabe — 2016-02-03T00:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-187988>

So for January ive been conducting an inadvertent N=1. Ive fallen off the low carb wagon and am back to eating carbs. I can usually make it till lunch before the carb cravings become uncontrollable. Here are my observations:

-   Energy levels are still very low, but sleep maint insomnia (4 am hot flashes) have become less frequent, although I still wake up and stay exhausted.
    -   Previously, re-introducing carbs has lead to a guaranteed week of 2am wakeups that gradually walk their way back up to 5:30am wakeups. (These arent roll-over-and-go-back-to-sleep wakups, they usually keep me up 2am-6am). **My theory** is that the metformin has slowed down protien gluconeogenesis so I am able to tolerate dietary carbs better.
-   Carb cravings are uncontrollable. On keto at least I could stare junk food in the face and not eat it (aka will power). Now if I leave the house its like im complelled to stop and get a treat involving processed sugar. Im not proud, but Im not going to beat myself up over it. Its brain hormones.
-   Weight is climbing, ive put on about 10 lbs in an month. I know the first 5 or 6 is "water weight" whatever that means, but that second 5 is straight to my belly. Its harder to breath with this extra weight pressing into my diaphram.
-   Intrestingly, this weekend I have started to get nausea and diarrhea with IR Metformin. Still testing to make sure its the metformin and not just that I have reintroduced peanut butter and frozen berry smoothies back in. Ill know in another day or two.
-   Bloodwork has changed significantly.
    -   Trigs are up (of course)
    -   HDL is way up, highest it has been boing back to 2012
    -   LDL is the lowest its been since before I started any version of Paleo
    -   Confounding factor: I did about three sessions of CT this weekend. Two sessions of 10 min in a 40f tub outside, and an hour in and out of the water on the river in very thick neoprene (but it was still cold as shit when I fell in). That isnt enough to raise HDL 22% is it? Ive been trying to get out and do more 20 min walks, but I havn't increased my exercise that much.

I'm attaching a screencap of the chart were I record my bio markers.

### Gabe — 2016-02-03T00:05:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-187990>

By eating carbs I just mean eating potatoes, carrots, fruit and some snickers bars. (Hey, remember that study that showed they have less of a glycemic impact than a slice of wheat bread, right?)

I am currently tapering back off the carbs, but from past experience it may take me a month to get back to "low carb" levels. The uncontrolled carb cravings and rapid weight gain are not something that I feel like I can cope with.

### caroline — 2016-02-03T01:51:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188004>

Gabe .....get back on the Leptin reset pronto!

You may need war more than 50gr.
That will get rid of the cravings pretty fast....
So - to be clear ......

Get out of bed and go outside -naked if possible and salute the sun. Stay outside grounding for 20 minutes.

no snacking between meals

### Gabe — 2016-02-03T02:36:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188013>

Hi Caroline, thank you for your help! ive been doing the Leptin Reset since 2014. I just recently fell off pretty hard (unexpected and uncontrollable carb cravings) recently and think it is important to post failures as well as successes. Well right now im having a lot of health failures right now, that is why I am here ;-). My plan is to get back on the leptin RX, but from past experience it will take me a few weeks to a month to titrate my carbs back down to where the carb cravings become manageable.

Usually when I have a cheat day or two (out of a month or two months of strict food) I get rebound 2am insomnia, which snaps me back into my routine, but I didnt get it this time for about a month. Honestly its been great to eat like a normal moderate carb person after years of low carb. **(Ive don low carb since about 2011, paleo starting 2012, and keto /epipaleo since 2013 ish)
**
Current theory to why I went off the rails:

-   In December I started trying the 2mm nicotine gum in the AM while grounding in the sun. I smoked for 12 years and had an extremely hard time quitting. I have a family history of alcoholism. I wonder if nicotine is bad for my N=1? Part of the problem is after a month I couldnt tell if it helped or not. I may try it out again once Im back on the leptin rx wagon.
-   Extremely stressful Christmas where I spent 6 days with my family, they all kind of shat on me for being low carb (aunt is a nurse, dad and uncle are skinny-fat type 2 diabetics that are sure they are treating it fine with medication and high carb-low fat diets). I didn't really have any successes to show for my efforts since Im at an all time health low right now, honestly it was pretty frustrating.

Get out of bed and go outside -naked if possible and salute the sun. Stay outside grounding for 20 minutes. - **been doing this.**
If you have left over protein in the fridge - grab it and sit outside as long as you can. -**Ive been doing BABs for a few years now.**

Leave 4 hours between meals and at least four hours before bed. **I kinda do this. Ive found I cant skip lunch. Here's a problem: If I dont eat a snack before bed, then I go hypoglycemic and wake up between midnight and 4am due to cortisol and adrenaline release to bring my blood sugar back up.**

no snacking between meals - **check, except because of above.**

Plenty of spring water **\- we installed a two stage water filter for our drinking water**

Wear blue blockers **\- got this on lock. I wear Skypers after dinner and Dewalts before bed. We have red bulbs all over the house to make it really dim after dinner.**

Get all electronics out of your bedroom. - **room for improvement here. I have a yellow screen on my phone and I use my Dewalts at night, but I agree I need to try getting it out of my bedroom.**

Make your bedroom as dark as possible **\- check, black out blinds.**

Have you read the C/T protocol? **\- yes, ive a lot of details about this in the posts above.**

### Gabe — 2016-02-03T02:55:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188015>

We're in Richmond, VA out in a quiet corner of the burbs. Its a townhouse, but were relatively low on the cell tower footprint websites when I checked. We dont have a neighbor on one side and on the other side our neighbor is hardly ever home. I cant see any other Wi-fi networks in bed with the Magnetico, so it is doing its job blocking that nnEMF. All our computer devices are hard wired except for our phones and tablet, and our wi-fi router is turned down to 25% power and we turn it off at night.

I left a stressful job back in 2014 because I didnt feel like I could work. With the irregular sleep maintenance insomnia I was getting a full nights sleep maybe one night a week, I just didn't feel like I could go on, I was very miserable.

I decided to cash in my GI Bill and go back to school, they pay for housing expenses now so that was a source of income for buying groceries and paying utilities, but nothing else.

School is kind of stressfull, so I decided to get a part time job instead for the summer. It was retail at a local boutique pet shop within walking distance. I felt better for about two months, then I started to feel exhausted all the time again.

When I went back to school in the Fall, I had a really hard time focusing and completing the homework assignments. (One stressor is that I spent about 4 weeks trying to get in contact with the programming tutor, because they assigned a new tutor that didn't know the material. So frustrating getting the run around on that for weeks).

I completed enough of the assignments to get a passing grade and decided to cut my losses and focus on my health full time for a quarter. This means since im not working or going to school we're a single income household and im using my savings from my last 3 months at work to cover groceries (cause DHA you know ;-)

My health insurance rates went up 30%, so I am going to the VA hospital now, which has been a great resource (Im a navy vet). Tomorrow I go in to see an endocrinologist in the Diabetes clinic and next week I have a follow up visit with my new Primary Care doctor. Im also seeing a psychiatrist at the VA, but she has made it clear she just does medication management.

### Gabe — 2016-02-03T03:06:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188016>

Here is my current problem: Sleep maintainance insomnia/early waking insomnia, extreme daytime fatigue, trouble concentrating.

So far at the VA the primary care doctor and the psychiatrist said that when they see patients with those symptoms it is usually caused anxiety. So they prescribe anti-anxiety meds (SSRI's), anxiety goes away, and the patients get better (or at least they stop coming in). But for me, I dont have anxiety. I left my job, I dont have kids, Im taking a semester off from school, I have friends and hobbies a supportive mate and two lovely dogs, there is money in the bank for food.

Im looking for a solution to non-anxiety related chronic fatigue symptoms. I did read the first blog Jack posted, I have the second blog printed out. I will read that and all the information on Cycloset that Gretchen sent me so I can figure out how to make a case by Thursday to why the VA should give diabetes medication to a guy who clearly isnt fully diabetic ;-)

Im not down, im trying to be really realistic about all of this and not sugar coat any of it. I have unusually bad health problems that I need to figure out how to treat. My morale is pretty high. Ask monica :-D

Like I had to be a foot away from it to pick up any movement on the trimeter and I couldn't detect it from the other side of the wall at all. ### Gabe — 2016-02-03T20:30:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188094>

So an interesting N=1 update. Ive been taking 2x 500mg Instant Release Metformin a day, one with breakfast and one with dinner, without no stomach issues. I did the Time Release for a couple of months (that stuff is worthless in my opinion) and then I switched to the IR with no stomach issues.

After about 30 days of re-introducing carbs back into my diet, those pills give me stomach cramps and diarrhea.

I had posted a thread on the Facebook group right after Christmas asking why I was fine on keto taking Met while my cousin was nauseous on Met. A couple of doctors and nurses responded and said they dont really know yet, but "it seems liek the people who need Met the most have the worst response to it". Another person said they switched to low carb for a few weeks and were able to tolerate it fine.

**My hypothesis**: Gut flora. There have been studies that show sugar can alter the dominant strains of probiotics in the gut in as little as three days. According to this interview with Dr Matt Andry, Met causes stomach issues because it produces an excess of serotonin in the gut.

[http://superhumanradio.com/shr-1723...-adaptations-high-fat-cold-effects-inuit.html](http://superhumanradio.com/shr-1723-linkage-metformin-beneficial-gut-microbe-genetic-adaptations-high-fat-cold-effects-inuit.html)

Im not sure exactly the mechanism of sugar loving gut strains vs fiber loving gut strains and excess serotonin caused by Met, and why the sugar loving strains cause intestinal discomfort, but my hypothesis is that they are connected.

I do know that off of Met, my carb tolerance is back to crap and now I wake up about an hour after I go to bed with a hotflash.

### Gabe — 2016-02-03T21:07:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188098>

**Hypothesis to the cause of sleep maintenance insomnia**:
Dr Matt Andry recently said in an interview that the 2am wake ups are caused by blood sugar disregulation. He said this is the same mechanism in menopausal women that cause them to wake up with hot flashes (his words, not mine, ladies ;-)

He is the second guest on this episode:

[http://superhumanradio.com/shr-1723...-adaptations-high-fat-cold-effects-inuit.html](http://superhumanradio.com/shr-1723-linkage-metformin-beneficial-gut-microbe-genetic-adaptations-high-fat-cold-effects-inuit.html)

Basically it breaks down like this:

-   person is leptin resistant
-   blood sugar drop after going to bed
-   blood sugar bottoms out.
-   body releases adrenaline to free up blood glucose from breaking down muscle (what is that mechanism called?)
-   Once adrenaline raises high enough, person wakes up between 2-4am and are hot, and blood sugar is a little elevated. If it is a women in menopause she is told it is a normal hot flash.
    -   (Naturopath Nora Gedgaudas mentions in her PaleoFX seminar that hotflashes are not a normal part of menopause. She says that when women are in keto or VLC diets, they have no symptoms of menopause other then their period stops) You can see her seminar here:

    -   I know Nora doesn't have all the answers, but she does understand for to fix blood sugar dis regulation with VLC which jives with the Leptin RX and she came out with an Ebook a few years ago that showed Adrenal Fatigue Syndrome was a dysfunction of the HPA axis and NOT the adrenal glands being 'weak' which means that "adrenal fatigue supplements" that "treat the weakened adrenals" wont work, since the problem is in the brain, not the adrenals.

My experiences:

-   I keep my room between 61 and 57f in the winter, and I have a thermometer in my room. When I wake up I check my thermometer to verify that it is me and not just that the room has gotten hotter. It is always me that gets hot and wakes up hot and sweaty.
-   When I am in keto, before a meal my blood sugar is in the 60-70 range. When I wake up with a hotflash, my blood sugar is always in the 90 range.
-   Sometimes I can go back to sleep, sometimes I am awake until sunrise, then I get sleepy and can go back to sleep for a few hours.
-   When I wake up in the middle of the night, my heart is pounding, but I am physically exhausted. Like usually even too tired to even get up and pee. I have an iron bladder now.

What is not the cause of me waking up at 2am:

-   **Room is too hot**\- I verify that it is still cold as shit in my room when I wake up. I use a ceiling fan too.
-   **drinking too much water and having to pee** I think Robb Wolfe mentioned in the Paleo Solution that this is kind of a horse before the cart scenario. If you arent "wired and tired" you will sleep through the night. If you are "wired and tired", then you will wake up in the middle of the night and have to pee. I wake up and im too tired to get up to pee so I just hold it. If I can get back to sleep then I will, regardless of how full my bladder is, a full bladder isn't keeping me awake.
-   **Worrying too much, or worrying about not sleeping aka negative mental feedback loop**: I did worry about not getting a full night sleep, then I had so many days at work over the years where I got by just fine on 2 hours sleep, that I stopped worrying about it and just picked up a novel and started reading. Did that help me stop waking up or help me get back to sleep? No. Why? Its blood sugar, not stress. When I quit my stressful job I thought I would sleep like a baby. Guess what? Didn't make a damn bit of difference. That points to the insomnia not being stress related.
-   **Not exercising enough** - More exercise makes me sleep worse. I do whitewater SUP (like white water kayaking), maybe 2 or 3 times a month now. We have a short little river run in town, im out there for maybe two hours, mostly floating down river with the current, or parked in eddies watching other people surf waves. Kind of mild aerobic with a little bit of short HIIT thrown in if I want to try catching a river wave. I have to really take it easy out there or I will sleep like crap that night. Its the opposite of what everyone else out there experiences. Other days I stay home all day and take a magnesium bath and sleep great, even through Ive basically been sedentary all day. I think this goes back to what Jack says (In the Leptin or CT RX?) about not exercising much and doing CT instead.

### Dia — 2016-02-04T03:03:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188135>

I was looking for an article I read a while back ( but can't locate), which indicated that excess serotonin, triggers an excess of cortisol , which in turn increases prolactin and estrogen. which can be a vicious circle... have you ever considered eliminating the trytophan, since it converts to serotonin? And as you indicated above the Met can increase serotonin as well. so might you be increasing serotonin too much? High serotonin in the gut can also cause gut issues, as some studies have linked it to IBS. Have you looked at your progesterone and vitamin A levels? Those two are important for sleep. Plus considering the hot flashes, it might be worthwhile

### Gabe — 2016-02-05T20:18:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188364>

Hey here is a question: Are there any detrimental side effects of taking 1 tryptophan at night as a sleep aide? From my understanding it gets turned into melatonin since I take it at night instead of serotonin. It was explained to me (by a naturopath) that since it is a "precursor" to melatonin/serotonin it is safer then taking Melatinin directly, i.e. it wont down regulate endogenous meletonin production.

Is this a fact, or more naturopathic wishful thinking?

I am taking it as an alternative to a prescription sleeping aid Trazadone, which worked terribly (the effectiveness would trail off after 4 days, but then quitting after 5 days made me extremely irritable for a few days afterwards, not worth it IMHO).

Im going to try to come off tryptophan. It seems like evening blue blocking/dimming lights and using a red LED headlamp before bed is just as effective.

I had back-pain and felt lethargy all of the time compounded with muscle and bone aches, espeically when I added extra sugars on top of a bad environment. ### Shijin13 — 2016-02-08T00:20:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-188497>

> [Gabe said:](/goto/post?id=188368)
>
> Here's another question: I have an MRI of my head that I just got from a neurological study I participated in with the VA. The study coordinator said that the Dr that reviews the MRI's didn't make a note in his report regarding my scan, so there are no "abnormalities" (im guessing meaning no brain cancer or growths) What can I do to get someone to take a more critical look at it regarding my chronic fatigue? Should I just take the disk into the VA and ask to have a neurologist take a look at it? What do I tell them for a reason? Just that I have chronic fatigue and insomnia and I would like someone to take a look at this to see if there are any clues? Everyone at the VA hospital has been great so far, but they don't want to do stuff without a very clear reason. It is becoming clear to me that I need a better elevator pitch then "Im exhausted all the time and I don't know why, can you fix this?" because the answer so far is to just bounce me over to a different department. Which means I should be able to get them to bounce me over to neurology if I give them a plausible reason to.

Gabe ask for a 2nd opinion on the CF. I will say bouncing around the VA is pretty common, but that's b/c they don't use an integrative approach. CF/Fibro was treated by Psych when I was being seen. But I was also seen by neuro b/c of my migraines. Not sure if Neurologists will actually read an MRI, b/c its not in their "wheelhouse" in the VA system - that's the radiology dept. Treat approaching this like you need to take something to Senior Chief, or Master Chief. You're gonna have to run the wickets to get to them. You know how to work the bureaucracy, and as tiring as it is, once you run the gauntlet and get in from the Chief - you'll at least have run all your options in the VA system.

I left the VA b/c they weren't as helpful as I needed them to be. I was too much of a square peg, being forced into around hole. So, given that gird your loins, and prepare to battle extremely hard for your health. I know its exhaustive when you've got CF or Fibro.

### Gabe — 2016-02-16T04:25:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-189156>

Thanks for the encouragement! I dont really understand what I am supposed to be doing there at the VA, since I dont know exactly what is wrong with me. I kind of thought if I told them "chronic fatigue syndrome" and "im exhausted all the time" I would eventually get to see someone who can help me. Im not so sure yet. So far I know that the PTSD clinic wants to give me an SSRI to "reduce my anxiety so I will sleep better". The only endocrinologists are at the Diabetes clinic. I got to see a pretty smart Dr who has written some papers about leptin and was impressed by my knowledge, but he said while he was happy to refill my prescription of Metformin, Cycloset was completely out of the question for me, because the risk of side effects were too great for a "prevention" case like me. He said all of my sleep issues should be addressed by the Sleep clinic. I explained to him that after going to sleep clinics for 8 years, all they can do is make sure your CPAP machine is working properly and send you on your way. The endo doc made it clear that he didnt believe me and sent me on my way.

I have no idea what to do now. I see my PTSD phyciatrist again tomorrow. Ill show her the article you sent me and ask her for Cycloset, but I think the only thing she can prescribe me is Sertraline/Zoloft.

I see my Primary Care and the Sleep Clinic on Wednesday. Im starting to think they dont have any experience with people who are tired all the time that arent suffering from some form of accute stress or anxiety.

Im starting to get worried here. Ive been doing the DHA, light, magnetico, nnEMF, and cold thing for a while now, and getting worse. I have been commiting to getting a full 20 min of morning sun now, and I usually go for a long walk in the cold after breakfast. I keep waiting for it to get better, but it just doesnt.

### Gabe — 2016-02-16T04:35:12+0200

Update: The intestinal discomfort with the Metformin is gone, my gut is solid again. Besides this weekend, I am off the processed sugar again. I just need to phase out the natural peanut butter and I'll be back within leptin reset paramaters. The carb experiment was interesting. I slept more regularly at first, but my sugar cravings were out of control, I put on 10lbs, (it feels like its all in my belly), random irritability increased, and now Im waking up with fasting blood sugar in the 105's up from the 80's. Most importantly, I was just as exhausted all the time on carbs as I was on the letpin Rx, which is what I expected, but it was worth it to be sure.

### Gabe — 2016-02-16T04:38:32+0200

### Gabe — 2016-02-16T04:47:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-189161>

Since I quit work, almost two years ago, I have noticed that Im not as "jumpy". I dont startle as easy. If I get less than 4 hours of sleep, the jumpiness startle response comes back, but a halfway decent night of sleep will make it go away again.

Does that mean that in Adrenal Fatigue terms, my sympathetic nervous system is functioning properly as my fight or flight isnt kicking in like it used to? It used to be I'd have weeks or months where I'd be really jumpy and could feel my fight or flight response going crazy when someone startled me, or I bumped into a corner walking in the office, or someone suddenly came up to me, or I dropped something. That happens rarely now.

>
> -   I keep my room between 61 and 57f in the winter, and I have a thermometer in my room. When I wake up I check my thermometer to verify that it is me and not just that the room has gotten hotter. It is always me that gets hot and wakes up hot and sweaty.
> -   When I am in keto, before a meal my blood sugar is in the 60-70 range. When I wake up with a hotflash, my blood sugar is always in the 90 range.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-02-17T04:53:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gabes-journal.17334/post-189226>

1.  ****Meditation can reduce anxiety: by increasing melatonin locally and globally in the brain.****

_There is very strong evidence-based medicine that meditation reduces anxiety by activating the **anterior cingulate cortex**, activating the **ventromedial prefrontal cortex**, and activating the **anterior insula**. Transcendental meditation is more effective than placebos, relaxation response therapy, EMG-biofeedback, mantra meditation, PMR, and other relaxation methods. Meta-analysis of the data is robust. Meditation reduces anxiety as effectively as exercise and can be done with “spirituality” or without “spirituality.”_

Drugs are not the only way to treat anxiety. In fact, they maybe the worse way to treat it in today's world. That is up for debate but soon you'll see why I say it when the Time series continues. Several studies have shown that meditation can make dramatic improvements on reducing anxiety. Meditation can be taught and practiced with a “spiritual side” or without a “spiritual side” . I personally like it one way over another. There is, however, some evidence that including the spiritual aspect of meditation makes meditation more effective in treating anxiety.

Unfortunately, patients with high anxiety have a low frequency of compliance with mediating because they don’t do it enough and do not do it enough in sunlight. This makes the efficacy of treating anxiety with meditation questionable in my view.

Another old study showed that aerobic exercise, 70% of maximal activity, and meditation had an equal effect on reducing anxiety. A recent randomized controlled trial of meditation for the treatment of anxiety showed a reduction in anxiety, a reduction in blood pressure, but no change in a self-reported depression rating scale. On functional MRI, the following regions of the brain were affected:

– activation of the **_anterior cingulate cortex_** _(ACC)_

– activation of the **_ventromedial prefrontal cortex_** _(vmPFC)_

_–_ activation of the **_anterior insole._**

1.  ****Meditation increases melatonin levels, which helps you sleep. Melatonin also prevents cellular senescence and activates SIRT1 gene expression . This makes it a great way to lower % heteroplasmy in a bad brain bathed in blue light. The problem? Most bad brains. are low in dopamine and melatonin so rarely would they ever think to even consider this as an option........unless they sit outside more. Meditation in the sun increase cerebral mass which increase all biogenic amines.****

Meditation is an epigenetic drug that can change gene expression in the brain so it can be used in anxiety to perform brain surgery without a scalpel in anxiety. Meditation changes gene expression of histone deacetylase genes. Those genes are HDAC2, HDAC3, and HDAC9 in peripheral blood lymphocytes in meditators. The major mediatory is WBC's melatonin and DHEA-S. These changes alters global histone modification marks like H4 acetylation and H3K4 tri-methylation in the brain even when heteroplasmy rates are higher than we like.
**_

# Thread: The Earth's magnetic field creates the Van Allen belts..........
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T03:04:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185514>

The Van Allen belts hinder the use of telescopes to study deep space.............I believe they were the cause of Neil Armstrong's infertility for one year when he returned from the 1969 Moon landing. This likely will never be confirmed by NASA for obvious reasons. In fact, the party line from NASA is that "they now believe" that the Van Allen belts pose no risk to biologic systems.

Why do I think this is bullshit? Electrons always affect semiconductors. Becker's work was critical in showing us that biology uses semiconduction in many systems like bone. We have documented proof that space causes osteoporosis now for 40 years from multiple sources.

These belts are caused directly by Earth's own magnetic field, which acts like a [huge particle accelerator](http://www.extremetech.com/extreme/162397-earth-acts-as-a-giant-particle-accelerator-creating-the-dangerous-van-allen-radiation-belts) to ramp these ions up to dangerous speeds. The Earth appears to act as a giant particle accelerator like the one that is built in CERN.

One of the many worries people had when first sending humans to the Moon had to do with the Van Allen radiation belts. This was true in Russia and the USA. These belts are layered, two-lobed areas of space around the Earth that have an **_unusually high density of high-energy charged particles, including electrons._** These electrons are well known to damage electronics by penetrating deep into a spacecraft and often causing harmful releases of energy in semiconductors or electrical relays.

When the Apollo missions sent humans through these belts of space, NASA simply had no idea what to expect since prior human flights had never gone far enough out to cross the fields. The astronauts zipped through unharmed. But one can believe you were unharmed when you do not know what too look for. Becker's work on bone semiconduction was done when the USA was getting into space. If you do not know where to look for an effect will you ever find one? This is why when I heard about Armstrong's issues and the other issues of astronauts and cosmonauts in space I pay deep attention. Today the Van Allen belts aren’t thought to pose a significant danger to living things so long as living things are shielded and don’t spend too long inside them. This is why the ISS has to stay out of these belts.

Remember that the Van Allen belts are huge, dual-lobed areas of space around the Earth filled with high-velocity charged particles - electrons and protons whipping around the planet at near-relativistic speeds. Mitochondria also have electrons and protons zipping around them but they are not as highly powered as the ones surrounding Earth.

Since mitochondria also have their own magnetic field, might they also control protons like the the Van Allen Belts do but at lower energies? Might this effect be important in some way to biology that we have not figured out as yet? I think so. Dec 2015 webinar and Jan 2016 webinar are behind this idea I have.

In 1969 we had no clear understanding of _why_ the belts were so dangerous to electronics. In 2013 we got the answer. The journal _Science_ published the answer in 2013: it’s the Earth’s own magnetic field that makes the Van Allen belts so dangerous. When I heard this I immediately put my thinking cap on for biology.

When I read why this happens I got more concerned about nnEMF in our ionosphere as we raise Wifi from 2 G to 5 G this decade. The cause of this effect around our planet are due to **_lower-energy electrons_** that give off just the **_right frequency of electromagnetic radiation_** (think light). With the Van Allen belts the devil was found in the radio portion of the electromagnetic spectrum. Mitochondria are also bathed in man made RF frequencies. It turns out the RF band is powerful enough source of energy to be detectable with a simple hand-held antenna and headphones, though that can’t be too surprising given the level of energy it can impart to particles in the Van Allen belts. Lead researcher in the Science article, Geoffrey Reeves likened the RF effect to hitting a tethered ball: He said “the waves have just the right frequency to hit that tether ball each time it comes around, at just the right time, so it goes faster.” Eventually, these electron tether balls approach relativistic speeds. This got me thinking about the RF frequencies around all of our mitochondria...............especially the ones in our head that sticks up into the RF nnEMF cesspool. I have a sense this is why places like Orange County California have autism rates that are off the charts compared to other closely related zip codes with less population density. Might the Van Allen Belt experience in NASA help explain modern neuro-degeneration and neolithic disease?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T03:06:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185515>

For decades it was believed that Earth had only two Van Allen belts, but just a few months ago the Van Allen probes [discovered a third](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=third-van-allen-radiation-belt-makes-appearance-around-earth), much farther out than the first two. It turned out to be transient, eventually being blown away by a strong solar shockwave. Still, as we become increasingly dependent on global communications technology, a detailed understanding of these belts of space will become more important. Everything from GPS satellites to research telescopes shield their electronics and generally shut down when entering them to minimize the chance of damage. It appears the sun's light causes massive changes in this third belt............this got me thinking about Young's double slit experiment and the details in the photoelectric effect.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T03:18:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185519>

Interestingly, there is actually a "_scientific proposition_" to actually _destroy_ the Van Allen belts to help astrophysicists study space with telescopes. This will be done with a program called the High Voltage Orbiting Long Tether (HiVOLT). This system of five 100 kilometer-long **_charged tethers_** _would be deployed from satellites and magnetically deflect the charged particles using radio frequency electromagnetic waves_. Believe it or not, this small addition of energy from the RF waves, is capable of disbursing them remarkably quickly. So what might this idea imply about mobile communications that use these frequencies within the ionosphere? The current HiVOLT projections are capable of putting the electron flux at just 1% of today’s level after two months of operation if they choose to DESTROY these belts. Let me ask you this..........are you OK with scientists asking NASA to use RF frequencies to destroy belts that nature built when it appears we have no earthly idea the biologic benefits they maybe providing living things below?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T03:29:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185520>

Many satellites and other orbiting equipment today, like telescopes, have to shut off periodically, though, to avoid damage in the belts. That's a problem when launching equipment is often the single most expensive element of a mission; science will not abide a field that mucks with its experiments, nor even one that makes them [ship expensive shielding](http://www.geek.com/science/geek-answers-why-does-nasa-use-so-much-gold-foil-1568610/) into space. **If** there would really be no downside to destroying the belts, then [why not just do it, **if** we can](http://spectrum.ieee.org/aerospace/astrophysics/hacking-the-van-allen-belts)? Notice that there are two "**ifs**" in that statement. The second is the easier of the two: _Can_ we actually clear the belts with our technology already? Yep............now the better question: Should we?

The new proposition from scientists around the globe I mentioned above claims that very low frequency radio waves could be used to disburse Van Allen protons. So ask yourself, where the hell would they go? CERN answers that............they would head lower in the lower atmosphere because of the laws of physics. Considering how the Large Hadron collider is shielded do you think this is a really well thought out idea by NASA and science? Do we really think as a species that clearing several helpful new orbital distances for use by satellites and other equipment should be done before we understand the effect on biology? Remember these decisions won't be subject to a vote of the majority of people alive on Earth. It will be made by governments without any disclosure from its citizens. Today, we know that radio waves have significant problems getting through the highly charged ionosphere humans live in. The ionosphere sits between us and the Van Allen belt regions. Scientists, however, won't to destroy these built to study things. what if this destroys our planet in some way we are not mindful of yet? Don't we already have shit loads of unusual disease present today that we cannot yet explain? They remain hopeful that powerful RF emitters could mitigate this problem for them. This idea seems very myopic for life. Satellite-based VLF radio emitters have also been proposed as the cosmic wand to destroy our belts........, but such devices would take too much energy for an orbital platform. That is the only reason I can find why this has not been done as yet. Do you think this is why Google and Musk have a plan to cover the globe with WiFi.........?

Might this be the way the Van Allen Belts will be destroyed covertly by governments. In this way they could blame a corporations for the gaff and no one could pin it on them? Might they know these risks and be preparing an outward strategy to get to the nearest planet in case they shit the bed? Magnetic declination leads to biological deuteration and tritoration. Not good.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T03:52:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185523>

Consider some of these things tied directly to the magnetic field on our pale blue dot. Earth's geomagnetic field makes the compass work and protects the biosphere from cosmic radiation from our sun, quasars, and supernova's. This magnetic field has existed at least three billion years, although it fluctuates in strength and at times reverses polarity. When the field strength drops too low, life on Earth is imperiled by radiation of all frequencies. The surface gets bombarded by things that is not normal. Substantial changes in the magnetic field happen as quickly as within only a thousand years at times, although stable periods of hundreds of thousands of years also occur. Temperature patterns within the lower mantle influence both the stability and intensity of the field. These things are know to have massive effects on the climate in the ionosphere. Complete geomagnetic reversals on average occur every 200 thousand years-- but the last one was 780,000 years ago. Massive changes in or on the Earth, including extinction events, follow a 26.6 million to 30 million year cycle over the last 250 million years. The solar system crosses the relatively dense galactic plane every 30 million years. How might destroying these belts affect us........and the mitochondria that are our antenna to the cosmos..........???

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T03:56:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185524>

Accelerator Mass Spectrometry (AMS) is a new ultra-sensitive single atom counting method used to determine the concentrations of the most relevant long-lived cosmogenic radionuclides on Earth for the earth sciences. Ice core data is built upon these devices. Might we use them to study the effects of Van Allen belt destruction before we actually do it? These isotopes are deposited chronologically in the archives of our earth, such as trees, polar ice, lake and deep sea sediments. This is how we discovered how the last Maunder minimum caused denser wood in the trees of Northern Europe so that violin makers could make the finest stringed resonant instruments ever created by man. By measuring their concentration profiles, which in most cases is possible with AMS only, information on past changes in solar activity, geomagnetic field and earth climate can be obtained over long time intervals.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T04:14:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185527>

James Van Allen was interested in hoya vine and nocturnal plants when preparing humans for space travel. Night blooming plants react to certain areas of the general nighttime background radiation in a positive way. Rather than responding merely to the absence of the visible light during night time. A possible explanation for these effects was put forth by Van Allen himself when he suggested that the solar winds compress into a small rounded thin layer on the daylight side of Earth and sweep into a long tail on the night time side of Earth providing both energy and stimulus for noctural biologic activities. Van Allen suggested to NASA that the Earth's magnetic field causes a positive electrical charge on the morning side of the boundary and a negative charge on the opposite of the Earth clocked in darkness.

Van Allen's belief was tested by many researchers by doing the same experiments in different locations between night and daylight. Van Allen suggested this to John Ott as an explanation of why the time lasted pictures he did of animals and planets varied so much by the light environment they were kept in.................

I remember this data because the light environment is what creates the species of free radicals that mitochondria make. This would link them to the magnetic field changes that Van Allen mentioned to Ott. Seem far fetched until you really understand the photoelectric effect and magnetic flux.............and the observed affects made by Ott and Van Allen.

In 2013, [NASA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASA) reported that the [Van Allen Probes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_Allen_Probes) had discovered a transient, third radiation belt, which was observed for four weeks until destroyed by a powerful, interplanetary [shock wave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shock_wave)from the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun). That shockwave was the momentum in that light. Momentum is something that the photoelectric blog gets into in some serious detail. Light is capable of many shocking things photoelectrically and this is one of the more interesting things it can do............

CITES:

1.  Phillips, Tony, ed. (February 28, 2013). ["Van Allen Probes Discover a New Radiation Belt"](http://science.nasa.gov/science-news/science-at-nasa/2013/28feb_thirdbelt/). _Science@NASA_. [NASA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASA). Retrieved 2013-04-05.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T04:27:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-earths-magnetic-field-creates-the-van-allen-belts.17335/post-185532>

From Wikipedia I go this gem that worries me further..........
The outer belt consists mainly of high energy (0.1–10 [MeV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electronvolt)) electrons trapped by the Earth's magnetosphere. It is almost [toroidal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torus) in shape, extending from an altitude of about three to ten Earth radii (_RE_) or 13,000 to 60,000 kilometres (8,100 to 37,300 mi) above the Earth's surface. Its greatest intensity is usually around 4–5 _RE_. The outer electron radiation belt is mostly produced by the [inward radial diffusion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field)[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt#cite_note-8)[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt#cite_note-9) and local acceleration[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt#cite_note-nature437-10) due to transfer of energy from whistler-mode [plasma waves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waves_in_plasmas) to radiation belt electrons. Radiation belt electrons are also constantly removed by collisions with atmospheric neutrals,[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt#cite_note-nature437-10) losses to [magnetopause](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetopause), and the outward radial diffusion. The [gyroradii](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gyroradius) for energetic protons would be large enough to bring them into contact with the Earth's atmosphere. The electrons here have a high [flux](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flux) and at the outer edge (close to the magnetopause), where [geomagnetic field](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field) lines open into the [geomagnetic "tail"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetosphere#Magnetotail), fluxes of energetic electrons can drop to the low interplanetary levels within about 100 km (62 mi), a decrease by a factor of 1,000.

In 2014 it was discovered that the inner edge of the outer belt is characterized by a very sharp edge, below which highly relativistic electrons (> 5MeV) cannot penetrate.[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt#cite_note-11) **_The reason for this shield-like behavior is not well understood.

And we want to destroy these belts?_**

# Thread: The Leptin Rx was built around the photoelectric effect........
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-was-built-around-the-photoelectric-effect.17340/>
