### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T16:33:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-was-built-around-the-photoelectric-effect.17340/post-185559>

So how much do you know about light really? Do you know that light has a split personality?

The first person to explain how wave theory can also account for the laws of geometric optics was Christiaan Huygens in 1670. At the time, of course, nobody took the slightest notice of him. His work was later rediscovered after the eventual triumph of wave theory. Is light just a wave or is there more to it?
Huygens had a very important insight into the nature of wave propagation which is nowadays called _Huygens' principle_. When applied to the propagation of light waves, this principle states that:

_Every point on a wave-front may be considered a source of secondary spherical wavelets which spread out in the forward direction at the speed of light. The new wave-front is the tangential surface to all of these secondary wavelets._
According to Huygens' principle, a plane light wave propagates though free space at the speed of light = c.

The light rays associated with this wave-front propagate in straight-lines. It is also fairly straightforward to account for the laws of **reflection and refraction** using Huygens' principle. These properties of light are described ONLY by the wave part of light. Particle theory of light cannot explain reflection or refraction at all. Do you remember that EZ water has a different diffraction of light at 270 nm? Think this might be important in some way you have not yet considered?

**It is also fairly straightforward to account for the laws of reflection and refraction using Huygens' principle.**

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-06T16:45:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-was-built-around-the-photoelectric-effect.17340/post-185560>

Is the sun the librarian of our mind? Is certain frequencies of light tied to thought creation? Life is really a thought catalog, a pillar of human creation. Our "church" is really our mind. We can choose to see our ideas as a cathedral of the human spirit, from this perspective. Collectively it is a warehouse consecrated to the full spectrum of human experience. Just about every idea we've ever had as a species had a residence in someone's mind once. The best one's were written down or memorized and placed into an oral story. The mind is a place containing great thinking, making it a sacred space when you think about it. This is why I like to engage other minds. When you listen to the conscious part of their life, and its multitude of choices and ideas they bring to you, you won't let life happen to you.....you will make life happen for you.

What happens when you bury the sun from your biology? Let us review a bit. Cycloset actually raises your AM cortisol spike that we normally see in a normal humans. I covered this extensively in my August webinar 2012 for members on my site. It resets the cortisol/DHEA/melatonin axis of the hypothalamus. Only light can do this for the eye. When the circadian cycle is off we usually see low melatonin levels in the eye first and then the brain. Low melatonin levels are consistent found in all epithelial tumors. In modern humans all epithelial cancers rates are currently exploding. Maybe now you might realize why. You still think the sun is the cause of all our epithelial cancers? Why is it that the further we go from the equator the more epithelial cancer doctors see? Might we have this all ass backwards because we don't have a properly light perspective?

Melatonin is the third most important anti-oxidant in the human brain behind leptin and dopamine. All of these hormones are light activated at some point. When melatonin is lowered chronically for any reason, we see a sharp rise in epithelial cancers. Epithelial cancers have risen dramatically since 1883 when electric light showed up in Chicago because of Tesla and Westinghouse for the first time. When Cycloset was approved for use by the FDA for T2D in 2009, it validated all my empiric clinical observations of what really causes diabetes and what is behind most epithelial cancers. I believe the environmental mismatches that have gone on for decades are the real etiology for the modern epidemics we are seeing explode in medicine. A lack of sunlight = more blue light = more free radicals from mitochondria = low melatonin levels. It is due to a modern human never facing a true summer of sun and a true winter of cold to replace dopamine which controls the release of most pituitary hormones photo-electrically. The loss of dopamine make DHA in the RPE less planar and this alters optics in the eye. When the eye goes the brain goes next because the eye is connected to the brain by the optic nerve, This light environment never gives the brain's mitochondria the appropriate sunlight and sundown stimulus it needs to function optimally.

If you don't control for the effects of light on circadian clocks your experiments lacks any biologic power in my opinion. This is why most animal experiments are worthless to medicine today. Is this controversial view point for me? It sure is. Am I afraid of being different. Not at all. The science of light tells me a lot. It explains why clinicians are impotent with the research we base decisions upon. The research community is doing poor experiments with no light controls and this leads us to faulty conclusions. The right thing isn't always real obvious to the rest of the world. Sometimes the right thing for one person is the wrong thing for someone else. And everything, in retrospect, is obvious to those who use forethought. Forethought requires adequate dopamine creation in the eye and the brain. Ironically you need UV light to make it. Researchers spend their lives in labs under blue light devoid of any UV exposure. No wonder we get bad data from them. How you think makes you great. Thinking is a function of dopamine creation in your eye. 99.9% of humans use hindsight to dictate their actions. Some of us train to see the unseen. Thursday find out how light and dopamine link directly to the Leptin Rx.

# Thread: Blue light blockers and UV light
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-blockers-and-uv-light.17342/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cygnario — 2016-01-06T21:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-blockers-and-uv-light.17342/post-185571>

I recently acquired blue light blocking glasses that supposedly block 99% blue light, however they also come with a UV400 filter. Since glass/polycarbonate block over 90% of UV spectrum, it will effectively filter the UV coming out of a mercury vapor bulb that I got for indoors and make it useless.
It's like a double edged sword.
How to get around this?
Are there blue blockers that do not block the UV spectrum?

### lilreddgirl — 2016-01-08T07:30:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-light-blockers-and-uv-light.17342/post-185732>

> [cygnario said:](/goto/post?id=185571)
>
> I recently acquired blue light blocking glasses that supposedly block 99% blue light, however they also come with a UV400 filter. Since glass/polycarbonate block over 90% of UV spectrum, it will effectively filter the UV coming out of a mercury vapor bulb that I got for indoors and make it useless.
> It's like a double edged sword.
> How to get around this?
> Are there blue blockers that do not block the UV spectrum?

I don't think there are... as far as I see blue blockers are usually UV blockers that also happen to block blue (and that fits our use here). Blue blockers are only for night time use or if you are spending time in a building that uses fluorescent lights (like an office building usually).

The UV mercury vapor bulb is only for during the day use. (UVA is ok at night from blacklights and some UV only specific lights) But I think the mercury bulbs are usually full spectrum and ONLY for daytime.

I'll also guess that also if it gives off UVB it's only for day time use.

# Thread: Hi from Belgium
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-belgium.17343/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cygnario — 2016-01-06T22:45:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-belgium.17343/post-185573>

Hi guys,

I found out about Dr. Kruse on that selfhacked (Joe Cohen) podcast while searching for supplements, and I'm really glad that I did because it changed my perspective on health and life in the most radical way possible for the better for me and people around me.

Whole my life I have lived in an EMF hellhole in the city of Antwerp, Belgium around 3 cellphone towers within 250m and next to a tram line. Also spent most of my life indoors and away from the sun, behind a computer videogaming my life away.

Couple years ago, after being diagnosed with numerous neurological disorders and cognitive decline after dropping out of college because I had severe concentration and memory issues. I got a blood lab done and the only thing they could find was low vit. D - 9 ng/ml. Meanwhile I couldn't even speak articulately or find the words to express myself because of all the brain fog that I couldn't get rid of. So I got put on ritalin/anti depressants and vit. D pills, lo and behold this accelerated the downwards spiral. At first there was improvement, it's like being high on cocaine, but then the benefits decayed exponentially.

Fast forward 1 year, I was on all kinds of supplements and drugs and I did not even know which ones helped if at all because all of them combined made me feel better.
Then Jack came along and told me my flower pot had holes and I was dehydrated and the nnEMF caused calcium efflux which severely diminished neuron function.

Having studied electromechanical engineering and basic quantum physics I am fascinated by seeing things through Jack's eyes, it's frightening.

Jack, I am infinitely grateful for what you did and are doing for the community. And to express some of it, I'm reading your blogs, podcasts, webinars, books that you recommend and sharing the knowledge and the bitter truths with family and friends. They think I'm insane for the lengths I go to indulge myself with your prescriptions and advice. I tell them that they need to start thinking of themselves and being critical and stop being so dogmatic.

Cheers.

### Eugenia — 2016-01-07T06:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comt-and-mfhtr-homozygous.17345/post-185627>

I'm +/+ on both COMT V158M & H62H, and MFHTR A1298C. Any special advice/tweaks on this?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-07T18:27:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comt-and-mfhtr-homozygous.17345/post-185654>

The ancestor of every action in life was born of a thought from an unseen force. That force is buried in your DNA and SNP's for a reason. Time 6 clued you into to why CBS exists.

The source of nature is a detailed, intricate and very considerate designer, whose work is nothing less than awe inspiring. And we have only scratched the surface in terms of discovering how things work. Earth is a beautiful place to be when you surf nature’s waves. The shame about the politics is that they often surf against nature’s waves, though, allowing technology to be built to destroy these waves and the antenna's in SNP's.

There are a dozen views about everything in life until you know the true answer. Then there's never more than one.

**The sun was here before food was present on Earth. Earth ages = 4.5 billion yrs. Photosynthesis forms the BASIS of all food webs has only been around for 450-500 million years. The time line is critical in where we should direct our attention. Our system is attuned and order to these origins. We seem to forget that basic concept of nature.
**
If you have the SNP's here is the key point most whiff on: Your steepness of decline will be tied to ubiquitin rates. "Well-controlled studies" will not mesh with your N=1 because without control of the environment, especially in nutrition studies, ubiquitin rates are never controlled and SNP's expression is linked to this exponentially. When someone with SNP's is really sick it tells me ALWAYS LOOK OUTSIDE OF YOU and to the light choices they allow AND NOT INSIDE of their cells for some biochemical defect.
**

This is why I get so pissed with providers who deal with SNP's data from 23andme.......they think, and make those with SNP's believe, they can change the inside of their genome with supplements.....and they can't. This is akin to the story of the KT event and why geologist did not believe the asteroid story for so long. When Alvarez postulated the sequence of events that occurred 65 million years ago do you know why geologist thought he was crazy? _Because prior to 1990, no one had ever thought that rocks could be changed from outside forces from space........thy just thought climate weathering was the key. His ideas made them realize they had to look further outside._ I am telling people that SNP's are not tied to endogenous breakdown in your nucleic acids or the way you work. It is tied to the environment you allow and via ubiquitination rates they become expressed.........that is when they are a problem. _SNP's treatment requires an environmental clean up._ The Rx should not be prescribing to do things the other way around trying to solve the SNP presence. It is similar to the KT event. It is an environmental epigenetic issue tied to an altered environment increasing ubiquitin rates. SNP just slow down epigenetic changes and this is why in environments with higher exogenous energy flows they get sicker faster than other people

**"Raising awareness" by testing for SNP's is like medical screening tests, neither is enough to make people healthier. They create belief that enrich the messenger.**
**

### Pebbles — 2016-01-08T18:43:23+0200

### PathFinder — 2016-01-07T14:21:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-grueling-path-to-optimal.17346/post-185631>

I am from Punjab, a fertile land with plenty of sunshine which straddles both India and Pakistan.

My main problem is chronic, crippling, debilitating insomnia for most part of my life with has probably shrunk my hippocampus. That has unleashed plethora of problems such as mental fog, mood disorders, porous memory. Lack of initiative and state of incommunicado has another problem which I am dealing on daily basis. Not being able to write down (thoughts) is another big problem. Have done everything possible under the sun to fix broken sleep but it eluded me, led me to conclude non-conventional thinking is what might be the final answer and caused me to land on this JK forum.

I have been reading the forums to quite a while it’s hard to digest highly technical nature and relate it my condition but I have established some baseline facts which I intend to share in my posts. Even though I don’t have luxury of all those sophisticated labs available in US and UK but I have pretty much all important labs here

Lots of n=1 to share with group with my experimentations with different diets, GF,LCHF, sauerkraut, potato (resistant starch), sourdough bread, Intermittent fasting and their effect on mood and sleep. By carefully logging food and sleep I have 100’s of causes and effects but its more like a cacophony of diets-sleep axis and not an optimally directed rehearsed orchestra which I longed for. So I have success here and there but it was never sustained, I never get functional or optimal and that’s the problem.

Right now low doze benzodiazepine and herbal concoctions keep me going

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-07T15:35:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-grueling-path-to-optimal.17346/post-185638>

Welcome........Pathfinder things appear hard because you have no dopamine in your eye. When dopamine is low so is th amount of ocular melatonin made. Without both your life becomes your description. Read time 6 which went live this AM.

Life is really a thought catalog, a pillar of human creation. Our "church" is really our mind. We can choose to see our ideas as a cathedral of the human spirit, from this perspective. Collectively it is a warehouse consecrated to the full spectrum of human experience. Just about every idea we've ever had as a species had a residence in someone's mind once. The best one's were written down or memorized and placed into an oral story. The mind is a place containing great thinking, making it a sacred space when you think about it. This is why I like to engage other minds. When you listen to the conscious part of their life, and its multitude of choices and ideas they bring to you, you won't let life happen to you.....you will make life happen for you.

The sunlight melatonin link is huge for you. You live in an area with massive population density. Population density can block light waves from hitting your RPE while you are bathing in sunlight. My latest podcast with Jason Prall mentions this mechanism. It is likely behind your zip code problem. Consider the following:

What happens when you bury the sun from your biology? Let us review a bit. Cycloset actually raises your AM cortisol spike that we normally see in a normal humans. I covered this extensively in my August webinar 2012 for members on my site. It resets the cortisol/DHEA/melatonin axis of the hypothalamus. Melatonin and dopamine are amines made from sun light in out eye allowing us to think well and sleep well. Time 6 tells you how they link to sunlight. Read it. Only light can do this for the eye. When the circadian cycle is off we usually see low melatonin levels in the eye first and then the brain to the pineal mechanism. It is always linked to low dopamine levels = brain fog. Low melatonin levels are consistent found in all epithelial tumors and in those with poor sleep = poor ubiquitination. I have a whole series on this linkage. Read it. In modern humans all epithelial cancers rates and POOR sleep are currently exploding. Maybe now you might realize why. You still think the sun is the cause of all our epithelial cancers? Might lack of a proper solar spectrum be why no one can sleep in a blue lit world with nnEMF from the use of technology creating interference patterns around your head all day and night long? Why is it that the further we go from the equator the more epithelial cancer and the more insomnia we see? Might we have this all ass backwards because we don't have a properly light perspective?

Melatonin is the third most important anti-oxidant in the human brain behind leptin and dopamine. All of these hormones are light activated at some point. When melatonin is lowered chronically for any reason, we see a sharp rise in epithelial cancers and in insomnia. Epithelial cancers have risen dramatically since 1883 when electric light showed up in Chicago because of Tesla and Westinghouse for the first time. Today sleep disorders are at an unprecedented level. No one sleeps well any more. This is what you we should expect on the road to an extinction event. No senses this event because they have poor dopamine levels in their brain. Dopamine allows us to sense and perceive trends in the brain. It also allows for optimal thinking. Your journal shows you have both. Do you understand why now? Consider when Cycloset was approved for use by the FDA for T2D in 2009, it validated all my empiric clinical observations of what really causes diabetes and what is behind most epithelial cancers and poor sleep. Look up insomnia and diabetes link on Dr. Google.............you'll be shocked. almost every T2D has sleep apnea and poor sleep. Why? T2D and OSA are blue light nnEMF disease that change matter in your brain.

I believe the environmental mismatches that have gone on for decades are the real etiology for the modern epidemics we are seeing explode in medicine. A lack of sunlight = more blue light = more free radicals from mitochondria = low dopamine = low melatonin levels. It is due to a modern human never facing a true summer of sun and a true winter of cold to replace dopamine which controls the release of most pituitary hormones photo-electrically. The loss of dopamine make DHA in the RPE less planar and this alters optics in the eye. When the eye goes the brain goes next because the eye is connected to the brain by the optic nerve, This light environment never gives the brain's mitochondria the appropriate sunlight and sundown stimulus it needs to function optimally.

# Thread: Healing your hormones begins and ends with light/dopamine.  WHY?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healing-your-hormones-begins-and-ends-with-light-dopamine-why.17347/>

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-07T15:12:30+0200

# Thread: Eating RAW fish from your own freezer?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lilreddgirl — 2016-01-08T07:58:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185734>

Is anyone here eating their fish raw?

Are you buying it frozen and then thawing it out ?

I eat sushi and sashimi when out, but because I've never done it at home I feel hesitant to eat the fish raw from my own freezer. I've been reading online and am concerned about parasites...

I'm buying supermarket salmon that has already been cut and vacuum sealed. It's in my freezer which says that the temperature is -6 Farenheit.

Is this safe to thaw and eat raw? Is anyone else doing this? Are parasites a concern?

### cygnario — 2016-01-08T09:58:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185736>

For me, I've always eaten most of my fish either raw or smoked/fermented, never had any problems.
I really think raw is the way to go, also keeps the most important nutrients intact.

### Inger — 2016-01-08T12:10:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185738>

Well I do it all the time... freezed or not.... I enjoy it

### fitness@home — 2016-01-08T12:37:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185740>

I have eaten fish from my freezer that's been thawed many times.

Whether you eat raw fish at home or at a restaurant there is always going to be a chance of parasites.

The parasite thing...if your stomach acids are strong enough it shouldn't be an issue. It was one of my questions in a consult with Tim Jackson.

### Martin — 2016-01-08T15:10:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185745>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=185740)

Martin

### lilreddgirl — 2016-01-08T20:40:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185781>

Thank you for the answers!

I'm going to try it  I love raw sashimi when out... so now I'll get to have my own...

### TerrierMom — 2016-01-08T20:51:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185783>

My understanding is that freezing kills the parasites. I am moving to a raw diet for dogs and the the PaleoPet vet that I see (he has a great book too, you can find it on Amazon, Dr Coghlan), in his book he says always freeze the meat you get for them and thaw it, to protect from parasites....seeems as though it should work for us too. There are countries where they are never allowed to serve fish raw that has not been frozen and thawed (Canada, for example, last time I was there anyway). And I believe it is for this reason..... I eat tons of sashimi. I also can buy "sashimi grade" fish at the local Asian markets (gotta love southern CA for those markets alone), but honestly am not sure what the difference is between that and regular fish.

### TerrierMom — 2016-01-08T20:53:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185784>

Here's some info : For fish that contain parasites, the [FDA](http://www.fda.gov/Food/GuidanceRegulation/RetailFoodProtection/FoodCode/ucm186451.htm#part3-4) provides guidance under their Parasite Destruction Guarantee. This states in part that fish intended to be consumed raw must be “frozen and stored at a temperature of -20°C (-4°F) or below for a minimum of 168 hours (7 days)”. So.... hope that helps.

### SandyD — 2016-01-08T21:10:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-raw-fish-from-your-own-freezer.17350/post-185785>

Perhaps this is the reason I don't get food poisoning from bought sashimi, but I get it from anything else raw, with regards to HCL, is this something one needs to do all the time? Or would the body heal the HCL levels increases gradually and one can come off them? I don't want to be relying on HCL for a prolonged period of time.

# Thread: My journey to feel alive
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 189
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-08T14:08:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-185741>

Been around and about for a little while now... time to begin the journey through my journal... the rabbit hole of me.

I'm 32yo - now 33
Single mum of 2 kids aged 9 & 6 - now 10 & 7
155cm, 48kg.
Location: North Brisbane, Australia
Occupation: Naturopath, Grad Cert Evidence Based Conplementary Medicine, also studying post grad bachelor of medicines management with professional honours in complementary medicine, lots of computer time.
No more retail, now working in an office as naturopathic technical support and technical writing. Lots more computers.
Also run a naturopathic clinic from home. Not any more!
On top of that I write professionally for a natural skin care company & magazines. This, not anymore, I write for publications within my work. Don't judge my writing here, when I journal I write like I think  A bit here and there, a bit there and here.

I'm very loquacious.

My goals, my thoughts...

My aim is to feel alive. Truly alive. That is why my clinic name is called Alive in Wonderland.

I want to feel better in every way, to prevent a diseased frail old body that cannot function. I don't want to be one of the old people that I treat who are sick, sore & miserable! I refuse! I have had the term 'fibromyalgia' thrown around because of flare ups of aches/pains/twitches that have occurred when I haven't slept enough or have drunk too much alcohol etc (I rarely drink, it only takes having a few drinks two nights in a row to get the twitches). It used to be worse, but thankfully I don't really have much in the way of fibro-type symptoms anymore, haven't had for years - not bad at least, since I separated from my kids dad. I just get a twitch in my thumb if I have over done it. I do get 'flare-ups' when I don't take my Mg for a little while. If I go without Mg for a week I am sore all over, achy, tight... It's almost like my body can't store it. Every time I try to go without it I wished I hadn't. I am shit scared of rheumatoid arthritis, I occasionally get aches in my wrists & fingers (and it scares me even more because my escape is to jump on my motorbike, and I need pain-free hands to do that). I have an addictive personality (FHx addiction, my father is an alcoholic) and have suffered depression & anxiety most of my life, but currently have few episodes when I eat right, get enough sun etc. Well, ok, I have my moments... but they aren't often. I don't socialise too much because I find small talk difficult. I don't like people judging me. I have major fears around illness because I spent so many years being so unwell (I was a chubby kid & teen, deep in the throws of depression, suicidal, benign intracranial hypertension & migraines daily) that I am desperate to never ever go back there. Thankfully it has been many many years, and the further I go the better I feel.

I have learnt how to find my own way through my own ailments by listening to my body and doing what feels right. I eat a paleo-style diet, completely gluten & dairy free due to IBS-D symptoms which are mostly abated and have been for years. I eat a doz oysters a week & aim for a high seafood diet, lots of protein & good fats, very minimal carbs, but more during summer which is where we are at in Oz. I used to be 20kg heavier about 6yrs ago, but when I stopped eating shit I stopped feeling like shit and the weight just shifted. About 2yrs ago I went completely Paleo and my body toned up more and I had never felt better. My skin cleared up a lot (I had adult hormonal breakouts), and now I only get the very occasional break out.

Now that I have the weight I am happy with I want to do better in every way. I feel I need to, I owe it to myself, my kids & my clients. I sleep with my curtains open so that I wake up with the sunrise. I keep all the curtains drawn in my house so that while I'm getting ready for work I can expose my eyes to as much natural light as possible. I eat breakfast outside about half an hour after arising, with a coffee. I then continue to get myself and my son ready for school, and head off to work where I work inside under fluro lights and am in front of computers for most of the day unless I'm talking to customers. I take my lunch outside when I get a lunch break, however I often don't even get a break. After working all day, I pick up my son and I keep all the curtains drawn for as long as possible to enjoy the last of the daylight hours here in Australia, by which time it's about 6:30-7pm. We have dinner by dim light and I keep most lights off except for salt lamps and candles. I have music with me almost everywhere I go. If it's not playing at home or work then I probably have my headphones in while I walk to do the banking etc during the day.

My son is my current focus. He has been diagnosed with ADHD & dyslexia, and is on medication and supplements. He is often tired (pseudohypoxia perhaps?) - he is 9yo & still sucks his thumb. We have overcome a severe fructose malabsorption with him ourselves that the paediatric gastroenterologist couldn't solve when he was 5yo. I am in the process of working with his routine to get him more sunlight hours to improve his dopamine, and desperate to get him to have less screen hours - he is addicted to computer games, yet he is also extremely fond of the outdoors. He is currently away camping and even told me that he feels camping fixes everything for him. He was born 'in the sack' and is a real water baby, loves the beach, loves to climb rocks and has no sense of fear. Actually that reminds me of Jeremy and his desire to climb. My son will spend all day outside running & exploring and getting dirty, he was designed for that. Unfortunately we live in a duplex with a small backyard, and he has no desire to go outside when we are at home. I have made a pact to take him to our local foreshore at least once a fortnight (he is mostly with his dad on the weekends so I only get the chance one afternoon a week as I work the rest of the time and we don't get home till late). He has drum & violin lesson once a week, and was having swimming lessons once a week that I have just cancelled. I am a little apprehensive about this school year as they have requested every child has an iPad. I am about to start using blue light blocking glasses with him & the cool thing is that he is totally open to all this. It's like he instinctively knows it's right. He knows he doesn't feel right so will accept any changes I make to routine & lights etc. The main thing I struggle with him is his lack of motivation to be outside when at home.

I am in the process of creating an environment at home that is completely hacked. I am aware of things like structured water, EMF and of course blue light, and am working our way through that rabbit hole. We drink only spring water, no tap water, been that way for years. WiFi is turned off at every opportunity, and most certainly at night. I crave fire like nothing else, so I am investing in a small suburban-safe fire pit for the backyard come autumn.

I'm almost finished reading the Epi Paleo Rx, and I have Going Somewhere on order (I have a big passion for the science community and what occurs, particularly tied in with my current post grad) and next on my list is The Body Electric & The Fourth Phase...

I'm here to do better, to help my son, and to in turn help my clients. I want to educate people as much as I can, but before I can I need to educate myself. Which is why most of my spare time is spent reading journal articles, books, researching.... but I'm also very skeptical about published research due to things like publication bias.

I come with a search for knowledge. Because knowledge is power. I'm looking forward to documenting my changes.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-08T15:49:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-185748>

Knowledge builds wisdom and wisdom is a beginning to hope. Hope that you curiosity ends your former beliefs built upon half truths from your training. Endings are disturbances for us. They are depleted beginnings purposed to be substituted and replaced. There is never really an end to anything in our education; all that there is are beginnings. Endings are abstruse, mystical and unreal to us. To the curious they are comforting because they give us beginnings that are promising disturbances of hope, which offer a new leash on life. Starting over neither denounces nor belittles an end, it rather soothes and consoles us by reconstructing the broken pieces of yesterday. It mends them by reinforcing us with courage like never before. Without courage no other action would be taken to begin again.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-08T16:03:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-185754>

Starting over neither denounces nor belittles an end, it rather soothes and consoles us by reconstructing the broken pieces of yesterday. It mends them by reinforcing us with courage like never before. Without courage no other action would be taken to begin again.

Thank you Dr Kruse. The depth of your words is appreciated. Very aptly timed too, I just posted something on my personal fb page about courage.

I feel that is what I am doing - reconstructing my life. And assisting my son to build his.

My training taught me a lot, but thankfully the nutrition content in my learning was focused on holistic nutrition rather than dietetics which I despise. I train the dietetics out of my dietetian staff. I was certainly taught that fat is bad etc, the standard food pyramid stuff, but take all of that with a grain of sea-salt, and I am building on what I have learnt to get me to the real truths (I've got a book called Death by Food Pyramid which I love).

Thanks again

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-09T00:18:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-185806>

### caroline — 2016-01-19T04:23:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-186702>

Definitely turn everything off at the wall! Can you turn your breaker off???

In my opinion .....Your son sounds extremely intuitive about what works for him - pay very close attention .... Maybe follow his lead? Ask him if there is a better place for him to sleep in your house - let him think about it and deciide.

One of the members here found the only place she could sleep was in her dining room. What about camping out in your living room?

I think your son knows what is best for him. Dr. k. Always tells us to pay close attention to the doctor in our head.

You say he has learning difficulties ..... But I am blown away with what he knows intuitively is best for him.

He is listening to the doctor in his head ......now maybe you have to as well.....

Trust him - it sounds like he has some profound insights in what makes him feel awesome.

I don't know how much you know about Jeremy? His Mom was told he wouldn't survive past the age of five - so, since they thought every summer would be his last, they let him do what ever he wanted, what he felt he needed or what he could push himself to do.

Did you know that Einstein was a railway clerk?

Yes Jeremy's story is incredible isn't it. Brings tears to my eyes just thinking about the story I read. His desire to be outside is just like my son. I have gone through so much to get my son well, he was quite a sick baby and it's what spurred me to study naturopathy. I feel like I'm studying all over again with this - I don't like not knowing things and it bugs me that both the natural medicine & orthodox medicine teaches you nothing about this stuff.

My son struggles in some areas, but in others he is way beyond his years. He seems an old soul, we knew that from the moment he was born. He is caring & considerate, yet can be wild & get carried away. He seems old & wise yet baby-like as he still sucks his thumb at 9yrs old and proceedes to talk like a baby quite often. He has an incredible desire to be in the army. It's all he ever says he wants to do. He doesn't know his biological fathers side but his great grandad was in army intelligence, and there's a long history of servicemen in that bloodline.

His intuition is great - but I have never thought of asking him where HE wants to sleep. I will ask him as soon as he gets home, refreshed from being away.

Thank you.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-19T10:35:12+0200

My pregnancy was very rocky with him yes. I don't want to go into too much detail though because what I went through is exceptionally personal & I feel not the thing to post about publicly. Regarding his birth though, my son's O2 stats dropped during birth. He was born by vacuum delivery still in the sac (almost unheard of). He was in SCU for 5 days post birth, fully breastfed until 20mo, dairy & gluten free for most his life.

I have a daughter who is 6. She's is fairly good health, not many complaints from her besides her 6-going-on-16 attitude. She spends a lot of time with her dad so I can focus on our son (they fight a lot when together).

Family Hx: My dad's a chronic alcoholic. His father had angina. My mum has had fibroids/migraines, my grandma I'm not sure but I know she aged poorly and was quite forgetful & needed a lot of assistance when she was in her 70-80s. Not a lot of cancer in our family besides my mums dad.

My sons biological dad used to have a problem of undiagnosed bleeding from the bowels, possible Aspergers. His dad is ex-army & also an alcoholic. Don't know much more about his biological dad's health history.

☺️

I do spread myself thin... I love it though as I'm getting closer & closer to answers. I've just been accepted as an editor for Brisbane Paleo and will be posting updates on their page weekly... time to share some of my new-found knowledge with the folks of Brisbane I think! I'm also just about to apply for a new job as a naturopathic educator & tech support. It would be nice to get out of retail. Not sure if I'll be able to still run my clinic if I get the job but time will tell. Also, being a single mum I am the only one who can pay the bills so the only way I can keep up financially is to work full time, see clients in clinic 1 day a week and write articles at night... The post grad study I do is to get me past that, maybe into research - wherever the rabbit hole takes me

After I finished my naturopathy I went and studied chemistry at Uni. So everything he says I can picture in my head. I love it. As I'm reading his epi-paleo book I read through with ease, and often catch myself wondering what people must think he means when he uses all the letters etc that I just automatically read & understand (eg. CSF/celebro spinal fluid).

I have seen that 'importance of newbies' thread. Glanced at it, read a bit. Then went on to other things because I didn't see too much there that I thought was important... I'm trawling through the Aussie Donut Hole thread right now... should be updating my CV haha.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-20T04:57:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-186880>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=186879)

>
> Your sister's health may have some relevance to you and your son......and your mom's and grandma's.....
>
> Did you discover if you or your neighbours have a smart meter?

His diet... So much to say! He would tear meat straight off the bone given half the chance, he loves fish, he completely adores fruit (which was hard when he had a fructose malabsorption) and he's great with salads & veg, especially raw cucumber. He's mostly dairy & gluten/grain free with me, but has some of that at his dad's. He loves things like a salmon & salad sandwich on oat bread with a ton of avo. Breakfast is always a high protein smoothie on coconut milk with plant protein. Always whole foods, never packaged or processed stuff apart from the occasional plain chips or gluten free muesli bar. I'm exceptionally picky... You won't ever see any YoGo yoghurt or Shapes biscuit in his lunch! Lots of good fats & proteins. He has always had a sweet tooth for fruit, and I try to limit him to more more than 2 a day otherwise that's all he would eat a ton. My daughter is the opposite.

I still don't know about the smart meter. Don't actually know what I'm looking for...?

I haven't seen a PM come through, it should show in my inbox yeah?

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-23T04:33:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187139>

Felt like shit this morning, couldn't get out of my fog... Started last night when I was at a friend's place who has a new baby. I was so drained after being there for a couple hours, I was surprised I made it home ok!

Took me an hour or so of being at work before it lifted - I just sipped as much italian sparkling water as my bladder would allow (working by myself) and after an hour of that & talking to customers I felt so much better... I'm so glad as that was awful! Felt like I didn't sleep, but I'm pretty sure I got at least 7hrs sleep!!

### caroline — 2016-01-23T05:26:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187141>

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-23T07:10:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187148>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=187141)
>
> So what was the cause? Wifi? Lighting? .....or you want a baby???

Haha... I love babies, the profile pic on my fb is of me with her bubba and my pupils are HUGE! But I don't need/want another of my own goodness noooo.

I think maybe I was just sensitive to the general energy. She's struggling hormonally a bit, bubba was having a bad night, also lots of tech & wifi going on. It's hard to se my best friend not having an easy time

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-23T14:40:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187161>

Still reading through the Aussie Donut Hole thread... interesting stuff there. Made me think of a couple things...

As a teenager I really wanted to put my mattress on the floor. I had a metal-frame bed, and my instincts were screaming at me to pull my bed apart and sleep on the floor. So I did, I took my mattress off and pushed the bed frame up against the wall. My mum & step-dad were livid. The complained about the fact that we can afford beds so we should bloody well sleep in them, "what are we hobo's?" etc etc... We lived in a busy suburb at the bottom of Mt Nebo. Actually we were surrounded by mountains. It was great there. They just thought that all the metal music I was listening to had gone to my head, but I can't explain the strength of the desire I had to get rid of my bed frame!!

Also.. for some time now, months actually, I have felt a tingling in my hand when I hold my phone for too long, much worse when connected to WiFi... I noticed this myself before I read anything about the harmful effects of it, and now I'm even more aware of this. It's worse in the same hand I get the tremor - and I've often wondered if that was the trigger for my wrist pain that the doctor warned me might be the sign of early OA. I'm trying to not hold my phone if I don't have to, and have it in a flip case when I am on it...

The other thing I have to add tonight is that I picked up my kids from their dads after work. In great spirits, they'd not been on computer or technology of any kind all day. Been swimming, played a fair bit. Got home & played for a bit, but eventually my son turned on the tv and xbox... He played for a few minutes before I had to stop him for dinner - and I had so much trouble getting him off it! He scoffed his dinner (which was awesome btw) in order to get back to the xbox and play. He didn't rinse his plate so I called him to do that and go have a shower. He just wouldn't get off the darned xbox, so after warning him I would, I went over and switched it off at the wall. He lost it! He played for probably 20mins max and was in the most foulest mood during and straight after playing. He had a shower then played outside, and all was good again. I made him aware of the change in his personality, and he realised it. He also went to bed - in his room - after staying up & watching Everest movie (about climbers) with his Uvex glasses, all other lights in house off, so will see how he is when he gets up. Usual bed time is 8:15 but it was probably 9:30 when I tucked him in (rare late night as he will be leaving for camping AGAIN tomorrow - they're skipping the first week of school to go camping... I haven't watched a movie in a while, it was nice to have some down time).

I'm looking forward to seeing what he is like in the morning and what time he wakes up. His blinds are always drawn right next to his bed, and he usually doesn't get up even with the sunlight streaming right onto him.

Also, lastly, I applied for a new job yesterday. Wish me luck!!!

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-23T23:39:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187207>

It's 7:30am on Sunday morning... I went to bed at 11:45pm as I was up late reading. I used my blbg though.

My son got up at 11:30pm when I was in the shower. He said he couldn't sleep because he was hot. I also realised I hadn't turned the wifi off downstairs. So sent him back to bed with fan on & I turned wifi off.

He was asleep at 6am when I got up for the first time, 6am is my natural waking time. At 6:40am I heard his sister get up and try to convince him to get up. He said noooo I'm still tired. But she convinced him & helped him carry his doona & pillows downstairs.

I had unplugged the tv & Xbox last night but when I got up at 7:30 guess what he was doing... Decided to let him play to finish his battlefield mission. He agreed to finish up quickly due to the problems we had yesterday. When he gets off it'll be quiet play outside.

I feel head-heavy, particularly my sinuses. Need water. I had broken sleep which is unusual for me. Realised I didn't turn the wifi search off on my iPhone which was on the other side of my double bed last night. Oops.

Second weekend he got me to the point of tears. I wonder if he will ever get better with this whole computer game thing...

### caroline — 2016-01-24T09:59:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187238>

Can you get him to keep a journal of how he feels every day? Would his Dad help him?

Things like how he feels when he wakes up, What he eats, how many trees he can climb in a day, how many times he can get in a river, how many fish can they catch, how much sunshine can he get every day.

Ask him to do a journal for Jack! Tell him another boy wrote Dr. K. a great letter ....and maybe he could too!

What a great idea! He is anxious about writing because he is well below his level - he is in Yr 4, his sister is in Yr 1 & she is better with reading & writing then him (she's way above though). His dad might help him... Maybe. I sent a message but I think they're out of range. Hopefully they go into town in a cpl days and he will get the message & start then! Brilliant brilliant brilliant idea. Thanks A/C!

### caroline — 2016-01-24T10:27:26+0200

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-24T10:34:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187243>

Yes we have Possum Magic. She's a wonderful author.

Young Pendragon has been diagnosed with dyslexia & dysgraphia, so he gets very intensive help at school. He has come leaps & bounds in the last year. But that's also come about with medication - while in not keen on it, it does seem to really help him. His grades increased almost immediately.

### caroline — 2016-01-24T11:17:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187251>

My oldest daughter is dyslexic ...So she was really concerned about her boys and their reading.

We had a friend with a son who had a serious reading problem - although bright.

They gave him comic books .....and he thrived!

My daughter had such a hard time reading and completing projects.

She loves anything about food and cooking and is a great cook and does food related stuff .....and devours cookbooks!

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-24T11:31:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187254>

The next thing we have to work on intensely is his ability to 'misplace' things. He has lost so many hats, puts them down somewhere and forgets about them. I had to buy 3 new drink bottles to go with the other 2 still remaining, just so we have a chance of having a drink bottle at the start of each day. He always forgets to bring them home or he leaves them somewhere...

### Lahelada — 2016-01-24T14:17:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187270>

> [Haley\_Pendragon said:](/goto/post?id=187232)
>

>
> Full moon too...
>
> Headed up the mountain for some chill time & lunch with little madam.

Haley,

I saw the same in the three yr old son of a student of mine. I touched his mothers tablet to look for the address of a bookstore and he turned red in the face immediately and screamed at me to leave his things alone. Mum came bustling over to tell me to leave it be as now is obviously not a good time for him. He has also made himself a card that says " end " for the picture stories he invents. He now uses it to tell his Mum to be quiet,too.
His Mum told me that she feels guilty and let's him get away with things as she was very sick with the birth of her second baby and literally not present for two months of his life.
He is a sweet kid otherwise,highly intelligent,shares his toys and sweets with me. But not his brother

### nicld — 2016-01-24T19:26:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187285>

> [Haley\_Pendragon said:](/goto/post?id=187219)
>
> Well, that ended badly. He didn't turn it off when he should have, started a big argument, ended up with a massive attitude & fighting with his sister so much that he's off to his dad's. Second weekend he got me to the point of tears. I wonder if he will ever get better with this whole computer game thing...

You are his mother and YOU can take it away......take it away. I NEVER allowed a gaming system in my house and we only had one computer to share with 3 people. My opinion is that he needs it gone for a month to redirect his brain (low dopamine) and then if YOU feel he can have it back set strict limits and consequences before hand. Say he can have it for just one hour 2-3 a week then have it on a timer so that it goes off. Let him know ahead of time if a fit is thrown when it goes off he will not get it again at the next scheduled time.

I really feel for you and how tough it is being a single mom trying to co-parent but you are strong and can do this.

Remember that we are here for you.

PS Caroline is a great support

### Inger — 2016-01-24T19:31:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187288>

I think it is a big mistake to give children technology from the beginning.
I see zero reason to do so. I would not allow my children anything like that.

I couldn't imagine my life if I hadn't stumbled upon Dr. K. .... And all of you....so much richness and colour and vitality and caring.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-25T00:48:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187307>

Thanks A/C!

A very aptly timed post from Dr K: "To get optimal stop giving a shit what others think. Take a limitation and turn it into an opportunity. It's not their beliefs that are your obstacle; it's your emotions giving them power over your lack of action. Your new beliefs must become a violent interference into the normal order of things of your life. You should always "listen" to your "parents and to experts". They'll always tell you what can't be done, and why. Your job is to show them the error of their precepts of truth by just doing it. Action, not words, shuts people up."

Actions, not words. And right now I'm all about the actions. I have always done & will always do what it takes

### PaulG — 2016-01-25T03:45:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187314>

Hi Haley

In relation to the xbox I have a similar issue with my 14yr old son who plays on his xbox one for hours on end even though we have a 2hr time limit. It is not possible to limit the time using the consoles parental control options as you could on the xbox 360. Its almost a daily cause of stress and resentment and to be honest I am very close to throwing it out but fear the negative causes eg being hated for a long time.. Anyway this is the approach I am taking next week when the new school year starts

**Step 1:** Book an eye test or doctor's appointment for your son. Go and see the specialst beforehand and ask them nicely if they would give a little speech about how bright screens at night and prolonged exposure to lcd monitors is bad for eye health and getting adequate sun is good for eye health. It's amazing how kids will listen to another non-family adult. Your son may smell a rat depending on his age but worth a shot. Its a bit sneaky and underhand but using a non-family authority figures could be very effective. Talk to the doctor or ophthalmologist how they tackle this issue with their kids..could be an interesting conversation and a chance to mention @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/). Don't forget to mention that Steve Jobs didn't let his kids have apple tech.
**Step 2:** Sit down with son and go through the many negative health benefits of too much screen time, prolonged sitting, blue-light at night etc etc.
**Step 3:** Get buy-in to the 2hr limit for weekdays and weekends (inc holidays, sick days) and even have technology-free days from ALL the kids and adults. Go through the consequences of non-complaince.
**Step 4:** Draw up a weekly roster showing the allowed gaming periods and pin this up in multiple places such as the fridge, bedroom door. Also include the consequences in **BIG BOLD WRITING
Step 5:** Buy a [digital timer / alarm clock](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B019Z8HM3U) and let son manage his own time. Get him to record start time, end time, duration every day into a log.
**Step 6:** Do regular checks to make sure he is following the rules

**Consequences**
Regular non compliance such as going over the time limit by more than 15mins with the excuse "I just need to finish off this level" or not filling in the log will have scaled consequences:

**Consequence 1:** Future gaming time is lost..this can range from hourly, daily, weekly
**Consequence 2:** Hard-wire the gaming times by supergluing the monitor power lead to a [digital power timer](http://www.amazon.com/Woods-50008-Digital-Programmable-Settings/dp/B006LYHEEY)
**Consequence 3:** If this is not working consider using a 'stronger' parental control system. See links below
**Consequence 4:** Swap out the xbox one for one that has better parental controls such as xbox 360.
**Consequence 5:** Sell the console and buy son a mountain bike.

Thinking there should be a whole forum dedicated to helping kids be optimal..I have had limited success with my own three teenagers who think I am a control freak and could use all the helpful advice and tips I can get as I feel I have failed to teach them effectively. @[Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) is xbox expert maybe interested.

**Physical parental control links**
[http://familysafe.com/](http://familysafe.com/)
[http://www.instructables.com/id/Technology-to-the-rescue-How-to-give-your-kids-th/?ALLSTEPS](http://www.instructables.com/id/Technology-to-the-rescue-How-to-give-your-kids-th/?ALLSTEPS)

**Software parental control (not for gaming consoles but good for computers, tablets, phones)**
[https://www.qustodio.com/en/](https://www.qustodio.com/en/)

Thanks for the amazingly helpful response! I don't like him playing it too much... My son really struggles with his school work but absolutely excels at computer games - he is exceptionally good, so for a kid who has very little confidence generally, it's been great for him to feel like he is good at something... I just need to manage it better and reduce any harm.

I love your suggestions. I'm going to get a digital clock to put a timer on his games. We actually have a 360 - I have no idea what parental controls are on this thing! I'm not really that good with any technical stuff lol.

I love all the consequence ideas. He has about 5 bicycles though, some here & some at his bike, lots of things like that, he does lots of physical stuff like bike riding, riding scooter, skateboard etc. I'll come up with something similar though!

The big problem I have with getting him off is that he knows he needs to stop playing, knows it's not good for him... But he wants to complete his xyz, finish this mission, get through the level etc. That's the most frustrating thing!

I'm going to do a lot of work and planning to get this in place, so that we can reach a mid-ground

### PaulG — 2016-01-25T09:34:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187321>

If you got an xbox 360 then its easy, you set up a 4 digit code and set how much time per day or per week.
[http://support.xbox.com/en-AU/xbox-360/security/xbox-live-parental-control](http://support.xbox.com/en-AU/xbox-360/security/xbox-live-parental-control)

maybe say to him that for a week he should do all the things you do and you play video games and see how he likes not having any food, clothes washed etc.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-25T09:49:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187322>

> maybe say to him that for a week he should do all the things you do and you play video games and see how he likes not having any food, clothes washed etc.

Hell yes. That's wonderful, I'm going to set that up! That makes it so easy. Thanks Paul!!!!!!!

Great idea about the not having any food/clothes washed... Being a single mum I have been wanting him to do more cooking, he mostly feels like he can and he loves to cook so much (things like bacon & eggs, steak, snags etc) but I have been too nervous due to him burning himself because he is a daydreamer. I've made a promise to myself that I'm going to have confidence in him to do it, because I really do need the help. Cooking is a life skill that so many people don't have... And he is keen to do much more of it so I'm glad to be able to step up and be his teacher for this, alongside his dad. His dad spends heaps of time in the kitchen with the kids - he can more than me as he doesn't work & lives rent free.

I got this! Xbox control ahead! Yippee

### Inger — 2016-01-25T10:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187323>

I do not believe in giving children a little bit of technology to "learn" to use it. I think it is bullshit.
I have friends that argue, the children need to be able to live in this world and that is why they need to give their children games and iphones etc so they learn to use them, otherwise they will eaier get addicted to it. I think this is totally wrong.
I am so very happy we had no technology at home as kids. We played outside all the time... I got addicted to nature.. and that blessing never left me. Every minute you let your kid sit in front of that crap you take away the valuable time it could have spent outside... or with you... talking.. reading.. doing stuff together.. exploring life... instead of exploring death in front of a screen.

Maybe it is because our own convenience (because we have to work so much to get the money to buy all the toys we do not need) that we let them do it.... but what a lie.
Instead we are getting all the issues you can read about above.
It is not funny. When it comes to kids a roaring lion awakes in me when I see them getting damaged and they suffer. I cant help it. It drives me nuts to read that parents think they do their kids any good with allowing them to use technology.

Kids needs to be in nature... they need to spend quality time with their parents.. doing stuff together... making live music.. art.... reading... learning about life
They need love! And real love they get from living things. Not from dead stuff.... fake gods

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187324>

Thanks for your input. Unfortunately I live in the the real world of single mum in suburbia with a small back yard who struggles to make ends meet sometimes. My money is spent on good food & water for my family, never on toys and unnecessary shit, that is what birthdays & Christmas is for - family buy that stuff for us because I can not and do not buy it. And I am grateful. My children live an existence between both nature and the reality of technology. It is our personal path. Sounds like you were lucky to have the childhood outside that you had. That's a rare thing these days.

We do the best we can with what we have and I will always continue to strive to make my children's lives better - within our means and in our present moment.

### caroline — 2016-01-25T11:36:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187326>

Very well said Haley.....we have to negotiate this new world of ours.

Of course the most important things are a loving family and as much outdoor time as possible ....but we do have to work and earn a living. We all have various and assorted family and work responsibilities and commitments.

We do the best we can given our circumstances. .....and we know better now - so we do so much better.

We are all a work in progress.......

It is not easy raising kids these days .....as Paul pointed out above ...Especially teenagers!

A lot of the young families on the forum have made significant progress with their kids ....but I am sure it is a struggle every day.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-25T23:53:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187368>

I think it comes down to this - I need to move eventually. Our backyard is a tiny shell, hardly bigger than our lounge room, and all that's there is a swing set. There's no trees to climb. If there was then that's all he would ever be doing.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-26T01:09:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187371>

Just to clarify the usage of the Xbox, it's usually 1-2hrs a week, mostly on a Sunday. The biggest problem lies within him wanting to get on it as soon as he is up and before I'm up so that I don't know how long he has played for etc. He knows he shouldn't be on it for long so will try to stretch it.

Young Pendragons routine is mostly like this:

Morning:
Monday to Wednesday: get up 7amish (usually me having to wake him), goes outside, have breakfast. Get ready for school, we leave at 8:15ish and its off to school.
Thursday: he has drum lesson before school. Friday: he is at his dad's and he takes him.

Daytime: school, different play areas on different days (due to his high needs his play areas are planned, he has a picture chart)

After school:
Monday: I pick him up. Depending on the day we might have a trip to the park, or have to do shopping, homework, I might have clients. Regardless, he either has play outside at home or park.
Tuesday & Wednesday: after school care. I pick him up at about 6pm.
Thursday & Friday: dad picks him up. Swimming on Friday avo. Dad usually gets him to help around the house & yard.

Nights: Always dinner outside. Often we aren't home till after 6pm. Play outside until it's dark, shower, occasional tv for half an hour maybe 2x wk, story time, bed.

Weekends:
Dads on Saturday. Comes to mine Saturday night with his sister, we have chill times, dinner outside. Quiet night, maybe a movie, Lego... Whatever's going.

Sunday: this is the hard day. I have both kids together and I try to take the kids out to the beach, the foreshore, up the mountain etc. Sometimes this can be difficult to manage by myself as the kids fight so much when they're together, so the outing is cut short. But no matter what I always try to go out. I need to get away as much as the kids do. Sunday is when he tends to spend a bit of time on the Xbox or computer, maybe an hour or two in divided play times.

There we go. That's what I'm working with. I believe that as he gets older and is able to be safe outside (road safety, lots of cars zooming around here) that he will be able to go exploring the neighbourhood and lakes on his bike. Right now he isn't old enough for that. His learning difficulties just makes him slower to be able to get to these stages than other kids, but it won't stop him. He's a great kid. I hope in time he makes some friends that he can meet up with & go riding together etc.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-26T11:19:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187382>

Today's random thought... CoEnzyme A... What can cause deficiency in CoA...

I feel much better when I increase my pantothenic acid intake. Brighter. Clearer. B5 assists CoA which in turn improves fatty acid synthesis... Which means better breakdown of fats... Including CoQ10, assuming DHA & fat soluable vitamins also. And boy does it help my skin.

Low ATP could cause low CoA as CoA is made from ATP, cysteine, B5... Oh wait I also took NAC today. Hell. I've increased my CoA with NAC AND B5. No wonder I feel so good. I wonder if my thinking is right with this & the mechanism of action here.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-30T10:54:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187668>

The kids are back from camping! I just picked them up after work today (Saturday). They nearly got wiped out in the massive storms but I got a message through to the ranger so their dad was well prepared, he's a smart guy.

They both seem peaceful. I got my son to write me some journal pieces about how he felt and what he did - huge thing for him as he suffers badly from dyslexia & dysgraphia. I have pics of him sitting journaling too, I'm so so proud!!! The kids go camping with their dad several times a year but this was a 6 day camp, one of the longest they've done. His writing makes me so proud. He wrote about swimming, swinging on ropes, about the hail and thunder, climbing trees and jumping into the water...

He didnt really write too much about what he felt. So I asked him to describe it to me:

"I felt like my body was all over the place and I could do what I want. I felt like the real me." After more questions he was able to describe what he meant: "I felt like my arms were free, my legs were free..."

Tonight he has also talked about "all this stuff" being "fake"(meaning tv, house/walls) and about the camping stuff being "real - like hunting and swimming"

My boy is very in tune with what's good for him.

### caroline — 2016-01-30T11:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187669>

Great stuff Haley .....you have a super star there in the making!

Joshua is so lucky to have such a terrific and committed and loving Mom.

I can't imagine where you guys will be a year from now.....you are putting in 150% and all of you will reap huge benefits.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-30T11:50:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187670>

Thanks A/C. I was blown away tonight when I asked him to go grab himself a tissue from my office - he walked in, couldn't see and when he realised he needed to turn the light on he walked out and grabbed his blue light blockers before going back and turning the light on. He also insisted on having his bag of hematite stones under his pillow as he believes they help him sleep.

I'm so proud of how far he has come, it's amazing to have him making these choices all by himself!

### caroline — 2016-01-30T11:59:20+0200

What a journey you all are on......each of you on your own path to greatness.

### caroline — 2016-01-31T04:27:57+0200
