### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-01-31T06:23:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187740>

Josh certainly gets a lot of it intuitively. He takes his shoes off when he is at the park or climbing rocks, most kids want to keep their shoes on!

I think that might be our activity tonight...!! Josh loves to explain and rant, it's the physical writing he struggles with. Right now I am surrounded by school books that need covering, preparing for the onslaught of school tomorrow. The school increased their play time last year by 15mins with excellent results. Still not sure if the blue light blockers will be helpful for him in the classroom... So much ahead! But the plan is for him to start walking to school by himself to get a bit extra sun in the morning. I'm confident he can do it. He's not 100% confident yet but im sure with time & support he will be!

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-01T04:07:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187827>

My son wrote a few words addressed to Dr K last night, took him 20 minutes to write about 2 sentences. I'm so proud of his effort, and he wants to write more so I'm not going to put it on here until he says I can.

First day back at school today for my kids (although it's now minus my daughter, she's home sick... funny how she swam in the creek and ate with her hands for a week but gets sick only a couple of days after being back in civilization - we think she picked up something from one of the other kids at Bunnings yesterday). Josh and I walked to school today, even though we were running late from Scarlett being sick. I'm hoping the extra sunlight time before school will be helpful. His new classroom has a kitchen so I'm planning to drop off a few extra bottles of water for him to be kept in the fridge so he can drink that when his own supply runs out. I'm confident he will have a good year this year!

### Richelle Jones — 2016-02-01T06:51:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187831>

> [Haley\_Pendragon said:](/goto/post?id=187219)
>
> Well, that ended badly. He didn't turn it off when he should have, started a big argument, ended up with a massive attitude & fighting with his sister so much that he's off to his dad's. Second weekend he got me to the point of tears. I wonder if he will ever get better with this whole computer game thing...

In my experience, no. My son is 14 and 10mths ago i packed away his xbox and iPad and he will never be getting them back. He was utterly addicted to them and played them at the expense of everything else. We argued every day, multiple times a day about how long he'd been on it and when he should turn it off...he would sneak back on it everytime my back was turned. School holidays were the worst! His attitude and behaviour once he hit puberty became more belligerent and explosive. His bad school report was a good enough reason to pack them away. He is still too lazy to get them back but at least he spends his days at the skatepark and not in his room. I could see him turning into some sort of lunatic killer. I am not kidding. He never had an xbox (or tv in his room) till he was 13 and I fretted over buying it and it was a silly mistake.

### Richelle Jones — 2016-02-01T07:00:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187832>

> [PaulG said:](/goto/post?id=187314)
>
> Hi Haley
>
> In relation to the xbox I have a similar issue with my 14yr old son who plays on his xbox one for hours on end even though we have a 2hr time limit. It is not possible to limit the time using the consoles parental control options as you could on the xbox 360. Its almost a daily cause of stress and resentment and to be honest I am very close to throwing it out but fear the negative causes eg being hated for a long time.. Anyway this is the approach I am taking next week when the new school year starts
>
> **Step 1:** Book an eye test or doctor's appointment for your son. Go and see the specialst beforehand and ask them nicely if they would give a little speech about how bright screens at night and prolonged exposure to lcd monitors is bad for eye health and getting adequate sun is good for eye health. It's amazing how kids will listen to another non-family adult. Your son may smell a rat depending on his age but worth a shot. Its a bit sneaky and underhand but using a non-family authority figures could be very effective. Talk to the doctor or ophthalmologist how they tackle this issue with their kids..could be an interesting conversation and a chance to mention @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/). Don't forget to mention that Steve Jobs didn't let his kids have apple tech.
> **Step 2:** Sit down with son and go through the many negative health benefits of too much screen time, prolonged sitting, blue-light at night etc etc.
> **Step 3:** Get buy-in to the 2hr limit for weekdays and weekends (inc holidays, sick days) and even have technology-free days from ALL the kids and adults. Go through the consequences of non-complaince.
> **Step 4:** Draw up a weekly roster showing the allowed gaming periods and pin this up in multiple places such as the fridge, bedroom door. Also include the consequences in **BIG BOLD WRITING
> Step 5:** Buy a [digital timer / alarm clock](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B019Z8HM3U) and let son manage his own time. Get him to record start time, end time, duration every day into a log.
> **Step 6:** Do regular checks to make sure he is following the rules
>
> **Consequences**
> Regular non compliance such as going over the time limit by more than 15mins with the excuse "I just need to finish off this level" or not filling in the log will have scaled consequences:
>
> **Consequence 1:** Future gaming time is lost..this can range from hourly, daily, weekly
> **Consequence 2:** Hard-wire the gaming times by supergluing the monitor power lead to a [digital power timer](http://www.amazon.com/Woods-50008-Digital-Programmable-Settings/dp/B006LYHEEY)
> **Consequence 3:** If this is not working consider using a 'stronger' parental control system. See links below
> **Consequence 4:** Swap out the xbox one for one that has better parental controls such as xbox 360.
> **Consequence 5:** Sell the console and buy son a mountain bike.
>
> Thinking there should be a whole forum dedicated to helping kids be optimal..I have had limited success with my own three teenagers who think I am a control freak and could use all the helpful advice and tips I can get as I feel I have failed to teach them effectively. @[Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) is xbox expert maybe interested.
>
>
> **Physical parental control links**
> [http://familysafe.com/](http://familysafe.com/)
> [http://www.instructables.com/id/Technology-to-the-rescue-How-to-give-your-kids-th/?ALLSTEPS](http://www.instructables.com/id/Technology-to-the-rescue-How-to-give-your-kids-th/?ALLSTEPS)
>
> **Software parental control (not for gaming consoles but good for computers, tablets, phones)**
> [https://www.qustodio.com/en/](https://www.qustodio.com/en/)

My son wasn't happy when I took away his xbox and he did say he hated me about a million times but he got over it. I have to be responsible for his child hood and his health. The effects of the xbox are magnified by puberty. I truly believe he was a crazy person in the making. He is much better now. Not perfect. But less explosive and violent towards me.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-01T07:15:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187833>

> [Richelle Jones said:](/goto/post?id=187831)
>
> In my experience, no. My son is 14 and 10mths ago i packed away his xbox and iPad and he will never be getting them back. He was utterly addicted to them and played them at the expense of everything else. We argued every day, multiple times a day about how long he'd been on it and when he should turn it off...he would sneak back on it everytime my back was turned. School holidays were the worst! His attitude and behaviour once he hit puberty became more belligerent and explosive. His bad school report was a good enough reason to pack them away. He is still too lazy to get them back but at least he spends his days at the skatepark and not in his room. I could see him turning into some sort of lunatic killer. I am not kidding. He never had an xbox (or tv in his room) till he was 13 and I fretted over buying it and it was a silly mistake.

I think we have had a win already. Perfect time for my son to get used to no tech when he got back from camping a few days ago - he didn't go on his Xbox once over the weekend! I'm super proud. He is making great decisions!

### Scompy — 2016-02-01T08:21:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-187834>

> [Haley\_Pendragon said:](/goto/post?id=187833)
>
> I think we have had a win already. Perfect time for my son to get used to no tech when he got back from camping a few days ago - he didn't go on his Xbox once over the weekend! I'm super proud. He is making great decisions!

Sounds like you're making great strides. I'll chime in a bit here on the gaming front, as it's been my profession over the past 20+ years after my Biology/Chemisty jobs. I help break this down for you into any specifics that you wish. My 7-year old son is also an awesome gamer, but I've gravitated him onto the PC with a mitigated controller (no rumble pack) using mostly Steam games on the Personal Computer for not only parental control, but also for no nnEMF because no console (assuming no Wifi in the home). Also, I can control lighting on the computer with good lighting and F.lux. Because of the huge variety of games on Steam, more games can be found that are geared toward problem solving and varying types of progression mechanics that drives learning analytical skills, but also for a quick gaming experience rather than longer ones. That's really the key if a person is able to control their gaming habits to reasonable time-chunks, assuming a person has previously developed strong life patterns and good time-management skills. Gaming or anything that can replace reality with a fantasy-world version runs the risk of getting a person to slide down a slope into bad time management or over-commitments to that 'thing.' A great example is the SCA: The Society for Creative Anachronisms. I've seen many people in college lost to that group that reinacts medival times and fantasy-based camping stuff. I've also seen some people in the SCA control their time with it and not let it take over their lives also. The similiarty to this SCA group and video games is relationship directly: other people logged into the game or reliance upon someone being at an event in real life. Reliance on other people is used as a crutch to control others. Many MMORPGs were designed this way over the past 10-20 years to get people online together at the same time and 'go on an event together' in the game itself. So it chips away at a person's notion of 'Reliability'. If a person isn't going to show up online inside a video game with possible real-life friends, then the real-life friend in London might get pissed off that another friend in Los Angeles isn't logging into the game at 3am. Many gamers cannot be honest to others about their real-life commitments and windows of time they have, so they often fall into the trap of letting the game control their time. This is just one small example, but I see it in many aspects of life, not just games, so it's why I used the SCA here as an example to show anything that promotes "life-escapism."

Here's my litmus test: If I find a game that can start and end at a moment's notice, then it becomes quite easy to stop playing at a moment's notice. Unfortunately, many game designs have longer chapters, sections or events that drives players to keep playing for extended periods of time, compelling players to keep going rather than being done at any moment's notice. Games are like food from that perspective of wanting to keep eating and not knowing when enough is enough.... A person with good discipline and !dopamine! usually won't let themselves be controlled. However, video games that give off stimulating blue-light with huge nnEMF around the head (Virtual Reality gaming is hitting this year bigtime), I foresee the aspects of addiction growing, not shrinking or staying the same, because of the biochemical changes to brain chemistry due to the biophysics of light and electromagnetism.

So if any person complains or gripes about playing games, why does it have to be video games per se? I'm a game designer, and I can tell you if you know what you're doing, you can create any video game on paper. The only loss is the hand-eye coordination and muscle-memory of the controller to the brain. Beyond that, any video game can be turned into a paper-version. As a matter of true fact, most video games have taken most ideas from paper games and reincorporated them into videogames over the years. The best example of this would be all Role-playing games. Although you don't get the pretty graphics, any video game can be simulated on paper and played with others. There's a huge and vast collection of card and board games available now,... it's just a matter of finding players that want to play them. The problem is the time-commitment to play traditional board/card games or the time it takes to redesign a video game into a paper-version. This is exactly where I let my 7-year old go creative, crazy and wild with 3x5 cards, graph paper, pencils, dice, cards, poker chips, tape, and color-markers. He's already invented 10+ games over the past year during his transition times from video games to table-games. And what I've found is that he now desires to play his own creations far more than the video games that paint him into a corner. Because he has a very large game-mechanics set in his brain now from lots of games he has seen, he pulls from what he wants in his creativity to make a game-at-any-moment he thinks may work. Just tonight, he blended 3 past games into one new game, and pulled me to the table to make some characters, roll some dice and try it out.

The big takeaway here is asking the question, "What is a game?" Does it have to involve a monitor and console? Does it have to be made by someone else? What mechanics does a game have? My suggestion... pull up a chair and play a game of Cards, Go or Chess. The old classics can be entertaining and also better time spent with another person instead of a random person online or some unfeeling AI. If that's not good enough for satisfying progression mechanics (an ongoing flavor of 'leveling up' and gaining new skill combinations), then using or inventing a tabletop RPG would fill that desire easily. A person that wants a 'twitch feeling' with a controller to brag to friends about dexterity is substituting it for playing a sport... basketball, soccer, heck even fencing. One could argue that playing a traditional sport develops far more overall dexterity skills than just using using the thumbs and wrist. As a juggler, I've laughed at some people that tell me about how well they've played x,y, or z game. It's usually a huge peer-brag-fest. Pride in a new physical skill by showing how good their hands are with a controller, then I ask them to juggle 3 balls or go bowling to see how good their technique is.

### caroline — 2016-02-01T10:14:26+0200

Wow Scompy, thank you so much! I sure do know how addictive mmorpg's are - I was addicted to WoW about 10-11yrs ago!

Josh has a PC and a steam account but same rules apply. He has not protested this new change at all, I think he feels the difference.

Tonight he got up after being in bed for 15mins, which is unlike him lately - I realised I had left the switch turned on at the wall for the tv downstairs which is right under his bed, and the switch connects to the Xbox as well so it was in sleep mode... Damn I usually switch it all off at the wall lately but had turned the news on this morning to see if they had an update on the bomb threat that happened at my kids school. I'm mad at myself for forgetting to switch it back off at the wall, but also a good learning moment to show me that the one night I left it on was when he had problems going to sleep! Might be a coincidence because we were a little out of routine tonight and it's storming, but I think it part of the problem.

### Scompy — 2016-02-04T08:07:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-188146>

> [Haley\_Pendragon said:](/goto/post?id=188052)
>
> Wow Scompy, thank you so much! I sure do know how addictive mmorpg's are - I was addicted to WoW about 10-11yrs ago!
>
> Josh has a PC and a steam account but same rules apply. He has not protested this new change at all, I think he feels the difference.
>
> Tonight he got up after being in bed for 15mins, which is unlike him lately - I realised I had left the switch turned on at the wall for the tv downstairs which is right under his bed, and the switch connects to the Xbox as well so it was in sleep mode... Damn I usually switch it all off at the wall lately but had turned the news on this morning to see if they had an update on the bomb threat that happened at my kids school. I'm mad at myself for forgetting to switch it back off at the wall, but also a good learning moment to show me that the one night I left it on was when he had problems going to sleep! Might be a coincidence because we were a little out of routine tonight and it's storming, but I think it part of the problem.

Yeah, I've played them all from the original Ultima Online days, Everquest, City of Heroes, Wow, etc., so I know first hand what sucks people in and why people get so addicted to them. I look back 20+ years ago and can nail down exactly what caused my isssues I had at the time... mainly playing videogames at 3am getting massive blue-light.

For electronics, I have established a pattern every night to go around and do a check before going to sleep. This includes shutting down every computer and device because the less electronics that are on, the less energy comes into the home while we're sleeping. Plus, it cuts down on the energy bill a bit too

Since my son is electrosensitive, I was pretty freaked out to see his eyes itching him and him tugging at his ears one day when he was sitting in a chair a foot away from the Xbox, and it was the exact same situation that it was plugged in at the wall and off in Standby mode, but was still in 'Search for Controllers'. It only took me 5mins to realize why he was doing it. What's great is that he's really been into creative card games, and I see that he's developing way more skills because he hasn't been playing video games. Only about once a week now he wants to play a Steam game, but it doesn't last too often because he's finding more flexibility with his creativity vs. a game telling him what he can or cannot do. This is at the heart of the attraction of MMOs because when you make a new character, you're given the illusion you and only you have that character. In video games, this isn't really true because there are far less combinations compared to board games or tabletop RPGs that have nearly infinite possibilities. I think my son is becoming aware that he is the only one that imposes limits on his creativity.

Damn I usually switch it all off at the wall lately but had turned the news on this morning to see if they had an update on the bomb threat that happened at my kids school. I'm mad at myself for forgetting to switch it back off at the wall, but also a good learning moment to show me that the one night I left it on was when he had problems going to sleep! Might be a coincidence because we were a little out of routine tonight and it's storming, but I think it part of the problem.

Last night my son actually still ended up sleeping really bad. He fell asleep after he got up that first time, but got up at 2:30am and came into my room - said "muuuuum I can't sleep." And he was quite awake too. He doesn't normally get up in the middle of the night. He wanted to lay on the floor next to my bed. Weird choice but I went with whatever he felt he wanted - he didn't even want his doona or some blankets or anything to put on the carpet.. It was really hot so I offered to put the air con on, and I asked what he wanted to put over him... So strange, he didn't want a sheet or blanket but grabbed a towel from my clean washing pile and put that over himself - on my carpet with a pillow from my bed, and went to sleep. He slept there until I got up to get ready (6am-ish). It was a strange night and he sure was tired in the morning. I asked him why he thinks he couldn't sleep and he said there was a white light in his room, that he didn't know where it came from but he couldn't stop looking at it. Very strange indeed.

### Scompy — 2016-02-04T20:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-188215>

Hi Haley, hopefully it's just a 'one-off' and he had a bad dream or was just a little dehydrated. My first thoughts reading above (if this was my son) would be that he's dehydrated, and 2nd, he might be sleeping nearby an nnEMF (probably magnetic).

Have you checked the magnetic EMF around the area of his room? For instance, on the backside by a wall? Sleeping spaces can be tricky depending on wiring in the home and what electricals are feeding the home. As another example, you mentioned the air con, so it's good to have that far away from anyone if it's running due to the magnetic EMF generation from it. Do you typically shut down electricals in the home while you sleep? I've even gotten into the habit of doing all of my washing/laundry and other heavy-electronic-use needs early in the day and never do them at night, especially after anyone has gone to sleep. I was shocked to learn that some of the dirty electricity in my home and the Volts/second coming into it right by the wall my son was sleeping by. The volts/second changes depending on what you're doing in the home too. I was getting 500 volts/second at times near his wall if the breaker to his room is still left on. Shutting off the breaker totally shuts down that crazy-high amount, but something simple like switching off a light on the light rather than shutting down electricity to the room made a huge difference in my case. I've even got a hallway light that when it is on, 1/2 of my entire home lights up with magnetic EMF through three rooms. I need to have an electrician look at that at some point, because I'm never going to turn it on again knowing that. Also, the harmonics of the wiring in the home can also produce RF-EMF too... this is why it's best to shut down as many electronics as possible when you sleep to minimize sleep disturbances. The most common mistake is having a plugged in alarm-clock in the room. Those suckers produce a TON of magnetic EMF and sometimes out to 4 to 5 feet away. I've got a battery-powered alarm clock and threw away all of the ones that are plugged in.

Does he get night-sweats normally? There are lots of posts here about 'sweating out light' here.

The last comment about seeing the 'white light'. Assuming that you've got the room very black/dark and no street lights or whatnot is coming in. If there was no white light in reality, that might be something perceived in the brain. I typically make a point to get my son hydrated about an hour before going to sleep, and have to think a lot about keeping him hydrated especially since his school now has WiFi in it and he needs to compensate even more for electron loss.

### Scompy — 2016-02-05T07:54:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-188307>

> [Haley\_Pendragon said:](/goto/post?id=188305)
>
> He may have been dehydrated, yes! I do switch everything off at the wall, and only turn them on when I need to use them eg phone charger, kettle, tv etc. but not the washing machine or dryer. In fact I do a lot of washing & drying at night as I get home late & work 6 days/wk.
>
> I have so many questions for you @[Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) - particularly about testing the magnetic EMF here. I'll PM you.

Yeah, the heaviest energy load into the home is washer/dryer (at least in mine). If you know where the energy comes in from off the street, you may be able to trace how it flows in the home. In my place, one of the walls to my son's room is the main drawing from the street and bends back through the middle of the house. For this reason, I've banned all laundry-use anytime my son is in his room because I see the volts/meter. Also, the back of the laundry machines often put out a HUGE magnetic EMF. Keep that in mind and know what's behind your machines (in my home was a pantry that I used to store my food and water, killing off the electrons in them). Same goes for what's on the OUTSIDE of the home near your sleeping space, whether it be a smart-meter or other electrical crap that could be penetrating into the sleep space.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-17T13:55:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-189234>

Latest update is that young pendragon is doing well, he is sleeping well but still tired in the mornings. Tonight he had a shower and afterwards told me he decided half way through to have a completely cold shower because I used the water when he was showering and it went cold, which he really liked it so decided to finish his shower on complete cold. He hasn't been on the Xbox in ages, and tonight he even offered to give me a massage! Wow, I felt so spoilt. He's so sweet. The massage was actually amazing!!! His dad is a remedial massage therapist and it seems he has been teaching him a lot over the last couple of years.

For me, I haven't had any twitches or ticks in a while. I'm feeling on top of my game. Just got a new job in a role as a naturopathic technical support & writer. I will be passing over my clients and not consulting anymore, basically closing my clinic doors. Sad, but this opportunity is amazing. Hopefully a better quality of life with better work hours! I'm looking forward to being immersed in an environment of intellectual equals, because i have felt very lost lately with zero intellectual stimulation in my day job of managing a retail health store.

I've been drinking sparkling Italian water from Aldi, and nothing but. My dry itchy skin has resolved since I have ceased drinking the pureau cask water. I'm eating more sardines, about 2x a week for breakfast. I am still having a dozen oysters once a week and plenty of other seafood (Australian prawns & scallops mainly). Happy days!

Hi hi. We were discussing the water on the Aussie donut hole thread, where we talked about the hole in the ozone affecting Australia's natural spring water, and how imported water would be better. Sparkling water is yummy. Imported = Italian. Sparkling Italian water. Better than the Australian spring water options, and Aldi have some at a great price.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-18T01:20:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-189280>

Just saw Dr Kruse's post on music. I don't ever want to lose what he said.

"Our eye clock, our camera vision, and all of our other senses like touch, smell, taste, and sound— rely on atomic interactions, which rely in turn on the interactions of electrically charged particles. Light can alter the electromagnetic interactions in all these sensory systems to tune it or ruin its melody. Even touch sensation, though for more subtle reasons, relies on electromagnetic vibrations and interactions. Since human senses are all based in electromagnetic interactions of some sort, they are all directly affected by photoelectric interactions. Music affects several senses all at once. Human brains take delight when a master musician violates an expectation in their melody an interesting way; this creates a sort of musical joke for our perception. Music breathes, it speaks, it touches us in ways that speeds us up, and slows life down just as the real world does, and our cerebellum finds pleasure in adjusting itself to stay synchronized. Music, like light is capable of readjusting our atomic lattice clocks in ways we dont understand yet. Nothing is ever really lost to us as long as we tap those memories by remember it using melatonin recycling. Music does this. At night the melatonin/cortisol redox could make and prune new neuronal circuits the brain made as it learns."

Music is my most healing mechanism ever. It has been that way my entire life. I was brought up on music, played instruments and passed that love on to my son who now plays drums (& a little violin). I have dated musicians, many of my friends are musicians, and it's a circle that I will never escape. It fulfills me. Music heals me, makes me feel alive. I have had my own idea of what music does for a long time, and personally i feel like it moves stuff within us and through us. I connect to it emotionally and when I'm having a bad day I need music. It releases chemicals in me. I couldn't think of living a life without it. As I sit next to my beat-up half size purple accoustic guitar, I reflect on my gratefulness for what music has brought to my life.

### Jenelle — 2016-02-18T01:27:39+0200

Sparkling water is yummy. Imported = Italian. Sparkling Italian water. Better than the Australian spring water options, and Aldi have some at a great price.

Thanks, Haley! I'm obviously new and have a lot to learn.  I appreciate your response. I haven't read the book yet, and have a feeling there are many things about water I do not know.

Also, could not agree more about music ~ I don't listen to it daily... So when I do crank it up at home or in the car, I can FEEL the difference. It's an immediate boost.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-20T12:19:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-189441>

I have been listening to the Jason Prall podcast with Michael Roesslein and the timing of it couldn't be more perfect. I have been one of those 'busy' people for so long. Well, things are changing. A few days ago I accepted a job as a naturopathic education & tech support which means I will be leaving my full time retail health food store management role, closing my clinic and not writing for a 3rd party. I'm basically rolling 3 jobs into one Monday to Friday 9-5 role. I will gain an extra day in my week and will have a whole lot less stress. I've never not been running around like a headless chook. I'm sure I'll fill the time with more post grad study and more reading (finally finished the last few pages of Epi paleo Rx and am onto 4th phase!). I'm reducing my workload and making the work I do more productive. It's time.

I'm sad to not be seeing clients anymore, but this is my journey. I can always go back to clinic work, my clinic will always be there, my clients will be passed on to someone I trust. Big changes.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-22T03:14:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-189552>

Taking a minute out of my ridiculously busy day to remind myself how I feel right at this moment. I feel sick. Physically sick in the stomach. I have a headache, and my chest hurts with sharp stabbing intermittent pains which I think is intercostal related, my head & neck are tight/sore - I feel like my body is zapping all my Mg due to stress and I have none left for proper muscular function. My head feels heavy. I feel dehydrated. I feel like if I were to have a coffee that I would get my tremors back in an instant.

It's my day off work today, but I have to go in at 3pm for hours & hours of stocktake. It's my last week in this job so I have a lot to organise. It's also my first day back at study for the year and I have printed out a shit ton of research articles & assignment papers (pretty sure shit ton is a standard unit of measurement... Seriously I feel like I have printed a forest) and yet while I'm excited to be doing this subject (the nerd in me had a massive mood uplift when I saw the scope of the papers I had to review in pharmacokinetic herb-drug interactions etc) I feel like i have no idea how I will get to it this week.... It's already 11am. I had an appointment at 9:30am, lady was running late. Now I have meals to prep, 2 articles to write and housework to do, and have to be at work by 3pm for stocktake. Then I have 5 full days of work after that. No free time until Saturday night, but then I'll pick up the kids after work and be in mummy mode until they're in bed. The only way i can see myself getting some of my readings done is late at night during the week.

This is how I've been living my life for so long. 1 more week of this left. Can't wait. I just haven't told my clients yet that I won't be consulting anymore, because I'm worried about trying to fit everyone in that wants to see me in the 5 weeks grace period I have to hand my clients over, and that's also a massive stress. 1 step at a time. 1 day at a time. Take it as it comes, move through it, the end is coming. Yep. Ok must get back to the day, it's been a lovely 3 minutes of down time lol

### Lahelada — 2016-02-22T16:07:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-189567>

A lot of these new life phases going around on the forum at the moment. Hang in there,the madness will stop.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-02-25T23:12:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-189789>

I refuse to be a slave to stress. 2 more days of this job to go.

I have been meaning to start colouring with my daughter since xmas time, I do a little bit with her, but she does it all the time and takes her colouring with her wherever she goes. The other day I was at work and walked past the book shop, there was something in the window that stopped me in my tracks! It was an Alice in Wonderland detailed adult colouring book - it's beautiful!! I bought it instantly. I have started, but it took me a long long time to do just a little bit because I'm such a perfectionist. I aim to do it a bit more frequently.

Update on young pendragon - he is going great guns. He has joined a running club at school and goes in early to run laps in part sun & part shade before school. He's coming home completely exhausted, but very satisfied! He's also joined the school drama group and will be rehearsing with them once a week - I'm so proud he did this on his own, it's a big deal for him.

I'm loving the latest Jason Prall podcast. The urban monk chat with Pedram Shojai is inspiring. I love that they talked about herbs as medicine. That's what I do. I wish more people understood the power of them. They are thrown around and taken & prescribed so lightly, but they really are medicines and they need to be understood better by the people on the front end of selling them. These podcasts have been very well timed for what's been going on in my life.

2 more days.

I think I have the picture attached! I'm so grateful if this works. The letter was written a few weeks ago by my 9yo son a couple of days after getting back from a week away camping.

"Dear Dr Kruse.
I like being in the wild a lot.
I like swimming and it feels like my body is free."

That took him about 20 mins to write, and was certainly not an easy task. He feels so good when he is away camping, and one of the other forumites encouraged us to write it down. He kept a little journal while he was away too that talked about how he climbed trees and went swimming in the creek almost every day. I'm so proud of him writing this!

My goodness it's not easy. Not seeing clients is the hardest thing about this transition because I have built Alive in Wonderland to be a safe haven for my clients & have supported so many through their health journeys. ### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-03-13T21:59:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-190777>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=185748)
>
> Knowledge builds wisdom and wisdom is a beginning to hope. Hope that you curiosity ends your former beliefs built upon half truths from your training. Endings are disturbances for us. They are depleted beginnings purposed to be substituted and replaced. There is never really an end to anything in our education; all that there is are beginnings. Endings are abstruse, mystical and unreal to us. To the curious they are comforting because they give us beginnings that are promising disturbances of hope, which offer a new leash on life. Starting over neither denounces nor belittles an end, it rather soothes and consoles us by reconstructing the broken pieces of yesterday. It mends them by reinforcing us with courage like never before. Without courage no other action would be taken to begin again.

I just read this again, it was the reply to my first journal entry here. It bought tears to my eyes. I have just moved into a new career, a new beginning with a new job and it is further putting my pieces together of my future. It is a scary move for me due to the depths of what I will be doing. I know, deep inside, that this is what I do best, and I have the courage to do this, the strength to take a deep breath and know that I got this. I begin again.

Oh and my post grad is kicking my butt for time! It's way more than the reported 4hrs a week - haven't picked up a book since I started!!! They sit next to me almost talking to me "read me, read me!" - I will get to you again very soon 4th phase! Same as the webinars, I wanted to do gold Klub for those... maybe a couple more weeks and I'll have time. Thank goodness for meal prepping with large meals. Trying to get lots of sun but not finding it easy with my new work routine. Eating lots of seafood. My shoulders have flared up, lots of burning pain. I'm thinking it's the increase sitting and decreased sunlight... it's frustrating me that I haven't found the time to get adequate sunlight every day, I have been leaving just after the sun is up and getting home on dusk and I try and go for a walk then. Very interesting discussion on fb about Mg. Getting ready for another big week ahead! Monday here we come.

### caroline — 2016-03-14T08:44:54+0200

Wonderful new beginnings! You got this! xo

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-03-25T01:11:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-191471>

First pay check from my new job - and Gold Klub joined! Happy days. Looking forward to catching up on a lot. Kids get back from camping today, they have been away for a week... I asked young pendragon to journal again while he was away.

I've been much more sedentary over the last couple weeks, it's driving me a bit batty - I used to stand for minimum 8 hours with a 1.5km walk in the middle of the day, now I'm sitting in an office and coming home to sit at my desk/couch to do assignment work till late. That assignment was just submitted (all 57 pages of it with 3 pages of references!) so I'm looking forward to developing a new routine that involves movement and walking after work and on weekends.

Currently drinking a mix of cask spring water and Italian sparkling mineral water daily, No exceptions. A dozen oysters a week. Full tin of sardines on low-carb toast minimum 3x week. No sunglasses or specs when outside. Exposing my eyes but not body to sunlight in the mornings due to routine (up at 4:30-5am)... Every time it hasn't rained when I get to work early I sit outside on a blanket in the sun, but it has rained a fair bit lately. Been pushing myself out of a good rhythm by staying up late for assessments, with jazz music playing in the background to keep me on track. Mostly using my blue blockers with dim lighting, looking forward to getting some Amber bulbs. I have a lot of salt lamps so I make this my main source of light when possible.

I was sitting outside at my back table writing this, enjoying the breeze, when my neighbours all poured outside and started jumping in their pool and mowing the lawn. Cue disruption, noise and allergies! Back inside I go with a super itchy nose... Suburbia... I got 5 mins of peace.

Anyway, I think that despite pushing my mind
& body to function with little sleep, I have managed to deal fairly reasonably with minimal side effects because of all the other good things I'm doing. If this was a couple years ago I would have my hand twitch by now and possibly a fibro flare-up. While it's not sustainable and conducive to long term health, I'm grateful to be able to do that and bounce back well.

### caroline — 2016-03-25T02:53:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-191473>

I was waiting for you to surface! it sounds like you have everything under control ....pretty much anyway!

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-03-25T03:11:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-191476>

Freaking amazing entanglement happening... The first webinar I listened to was January dopamine... So far I have gone back and played the last 15mins 3 times. It was only yesterday that I was getting down into the details of a cancer seminar from 2012, and while I was watching it at work I got a call to assist with a cancer case. THAT blew my mind because of the timing of the call, then today I chose that as my first webinar and it connects the dopamine-oncogenic link... UNBELIEVABLE. Just finished watching it. Want to watch it again and again. Also, it's now 11:11, my favourite time.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-03-27T06:47:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-191573>

It's been a great couple of days. Building back my dulled spirit with some sunshine and seafood with special free time by the water with someone who is on my wavelength... we talked about UV and wrinkles and iron and dopamine... for hours. I'm finding that I get frustrated easily by people who just ignore the truth, they know it, they have been shown it, they may have even asked for it, but then they choose to ignore it.
A while back Jack said something to me about half truths in my profession. The more I delve into my postgrad, the more I'm finding that investigating half truths is exactly what my current studies are all about. Bad science exists in the natural medicine field as well as the pharmaceutical industry, it's just that the pharmaceutical industry has buried theirs better. It is frustrating but enlivening to uncover truth. Some research papers have been quoted and referenced, referenced again, been part of reviews, round and round it goes, but if you go back to the research it was so poor that the findings are unable to conclude accurate results - yet that's what everyone quotes. Round and round it goes. Down the hole and back again. Anyway, I digress...
I feel good, I feel alive. That's exactly what I want. I've had time to repair and regenerate. My kids just got back from a week away camping and the spark in their eyes is beautiful.

Got to work early so I've been sitting in the sun in a park nearby, while I read Going Somewhere.

I'm looking at getting some Blutechs soon as all I do in my new job is stare at a computer. Thinking of the other things I can do at my desk. I have wondered if Quartz would be helpful for the UV but there really isn't a lot getting in - while we have big windows with lots of 'sunlight' getting in, it's all filtered through the glass. Im considering getting some orgonite. We have lots of plants around on our desks which is great. I want to give mine a name.

Life is good. Getting lots of natural light in the mornings (well my eyes are, working on the skin exposure).

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-04-03T14:26:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-191958>

I finally sent my son's blue light blockers over to his dads... he tucked them in the boot of his motorbike and rode over there in the beautiful morning sun, so that he could use them if he played the computer today. His dad messaged me when he got there to ask if I was for real. I said yes! And I explained why... then he said "ok fair enough, but you know you would sound insane to most people?" Indeed, that's because most people are blind to it and don't want to know. He is a very intelligent guy so at least he understood my reasoning and is happy to let him use them. I may invest in some Bluetech's for Young Pendragon also.

The kids have gone away camping AGAIN, for a week. I loaded them up with organic fruit (it's still hot weather here in Brisbane) and lots of tins of salmon, sardines, herrings etc. Also got their dad to tuck a surprise packed of fresh Tasmanian Smoked Salmon in the esky. The kids will love that!! My son has been very cuddly lately, he keeps coming up to me for cuddles and won't let go. I love it. He's got his little writing journal again.

Meanwhile I'm going to go check out the EMF Rx.

Oh, and good news - I'm finally listed here as Gold! It's official!!

### caroline — 2016-04-04T05:48:42+0300

all good things come to people who wait......

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-04-05T12:09:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-192077>

My memory recall has been so much better over the last several months. I've never had memory problems, but I'm finding that it seems like I have increased brain clarity...

Interesting the way things work out - I was thankfully able to understand part of what Dr K was talking about in this months webinar (certainly not all, but small parts of it!) and I truly believe I owe it all to studying chemistry at uni. I did a bit of chem at college for my naturopathy, but a couple years later I enrolled in a Uni degree and the first subject was chemistry. I did that subject but then decided not to continue the degree. I always thought that was such wasted time - until now. If I hadn't studied that then I wouldn't have an understanding about electrons/photons/neutrons, the periodic table etc etc, because the small amount of chem we did at college was so basic, it never sunk in. Now I'm grateful I enrolled in that Uni course! Everything happens for a reason

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-05-23T23:32:22+0300

Wow it's been a crazy couple of months. Just submitted my last assessment for the semester on the weekend, then to celebrate I took the family down to the beach for the day. So good!

So.... I have a man in my life now, we have been discussing moving to somewhere with more land away from the densely populated rabbit warren where I live. @[Jenny S](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12281/) guess where we were looking at property on the weekend?!

Can't wait to get back to reading my books and blogs. Catching up on the last of the May webinar now.

I'm so tired every day! Need sunlight! It's been making us all feel stir crazy. We have our plants with us but that's the best it gets right now. In other news, I have been diagnosed with superventricular tachycardia and an essential tremor. Doc prescribed a beta blocker, which is what they tried to put me on 2-3 yrs ago. While it's hard to come to terms with this because my past is still catching up with me, the reality is that if I hadn't done so much work with my health over the last 10 years that the outcome would likely have been much worse.

It's getting very cold here in Bris now, the winds are extremely icy and I am really missing the warm sun! It rained most of the weekend and next weekend I have a 3 day congress, so there's still ages until I can get a good amount of time in the sun.

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-10-18T01:29:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-202996>

Thanks everyone, it's lovely to be back. Currently this is what I'm doing...

My drive to work takes about 50+ minutes. I drive 2/3 of the way with my window down, even if it's just a little, to let the morning light in. I close the window for a short period of time when I hit the motorway. I also sit outside with my feet on the ground when I eat breakfast and go have lunch outside on the grass at work. Now that I'm finished my study for a while, I'll be hitting the beaches at least once a fortnight (Bribie, Noosa etc), and every other fortnight will probably head to Redcliffe for some sun and seafood.

Speaking of seafood, I have sardines for breakfast several days a week plus as much seafood as I can other times during the week.

More than half the water I drink is Italian sourced (still the same SanBenedetto sparkling water from Aldi!) and the rest is pure water (Australian unfortunately, but no fluoride).

I have most lights off at night except for the salt lamps around the house and turn all switches off at the walls (tv, washing machine, dryer etc, particularly overnight). I open up curtains and windows in the house as soon as I get up.

Doing my best to increase my quantum yield but always more I can do. Certainly more I need to do with my autoimmune.

I'm now getting into cell/mobile phones and interested in learning more about our standard frequency...

And reading, podcasts, more reading, more podcasts...

I hope you are all well, looking forward on catching up on the last few months of webinars! x

### Haley_Pendragon — 2016-11-29T23:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-204513>

Just listened to the MRI podcast Jack did with Ben Greenfield - freaking interesting. The tattoo thing has sunk in now, I finally understand! Also very interesting about the effect one can achieve with TENS. I'm going to go back and read Time 25...

Also trying to make headway on Cosmosapiens right now.

I've been getting acupuncture for the last couple of weeks and my joint pain has significantly decreased to a point that I haven't been for months. I think acupuncture, combined with all my life hacks, will finally bring me to a point of (hopefully) permanently reversing my pain! So far it seems that way! In saying that however, I think that I will see the greatest benefit when I can move out of the city to a place where I can't pick up 10 wifi's... that will come in time

### Haley_Pendragon — 2017-01-10T00:28:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206446>

My partner was checking a fuse the other day and told me that he didn't realise we had a smart metre - I said that last time I looked we didn't! Turns out that smart metres have started to be rolled out across Brisbane, since mid last year in fact... and may be a coincidence but that's when my autoimmune stuff started getting bad and led me to further investigations and a partial diagnosis... The metre was installed without any notice, questions or permissions. If I had known they were starting to be installed I would have locked that metre box and put signs up, too late now though.

Not only do I have one, but turns out I actually have THREE smart metres in my electrical box as I'm part of a duplex with 3 units and the box for all 3 units is on the outside of my front office. I feel deflated and frustrated, however am doing what I can to negate and conquer. I have wrapped each one in 3 layers of alfoil. Looks like I can't get a smart metre blocking box around them as they're too close together for one to fit (plus an expensive exercise for 3!). Since wrapping them my partner believes his tinnitus has reduced, which is excellent. I'm keener to move now more than ever and we are looking at land and house designs, discussing with companies in the area about options.

Apart from that, I'm just doing same old cold Italian water, sardines and other seafood, light exposure as much as I can during the day (open up the house when I get up, drive with my windows down on the way to work, go outside on my break) and avoid bright lights at night. The latter point is another issue where we live as we like to sit on our patio at dusk and at night with no lights on, just moonlight, however the neighbours a few metres away have a pool and spend hours outside at night with big bright lights on outside. Again, can't wait to move to get away!

### Jack Kruse — 2017-01-10T00:47:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206460>

Most people never test, they just assume.........HUGE MISTAKE. You must be organized in your mito-hacks.

Human mitohacking creates knowledge but that knowledge is never organized to lead to wisdom. New knowledge is always naive and self-confident; several older members here fell prey to it. It causes many problems causing them to believe that it will immediately change things in their life once they or the world recognizes it. NOT TRUE. That is the reason why the forces of scientific knowledge are so poorly organized by the medical literature. On the other hand, the science is cynic and skeptic, sly and fantastically organized on the surface. It never ever has the illusion of the ability to stand on its own feet and to win in a fair competition with nature's laws. That is why so many unripe minds are ready to use any kind of means without hesitation. And of course it does - under the banners of the most noble ideas and this is why people who rely on it often fail miserably.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-01-10T00:48:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206461>

At the banquet table of nature, there are no reserved seats for any living thing. You get what you can take, and you keep what you can hold. If you can't take anything, you won't get anything, and if you can't hold anything, you won't keep anything. And you can't take anything without circadian organization. Simple.

Thanks Jack. Trying to reduce the amount of electrons I lose by grounding and improving mitochondrial health, and filling myself with foods and light that will give me plenty. Both myself and the kids

### Haley_Pendragon — 2017-01-10T04:50:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206483>

Interesting - we have a cordless phone too for my son to use in emergencies, but we rarely use it. Our wifi is turned off at all times, EXCEPT for the period that we need to use it for Internet, then immediately unplug it. All the family knows this and do the same. I also avoid putting food and water next to the wifi. My son knows if I'm on my computer at night or of the tv is t switched off at the wall as he cant go to sleep (Xbox connected to same electrical point which stays in sleep mode unless switched off at the wall). Thankfully I'm rarely on my laptop after dark and only have the tv on at the wall if I've forgotten to switch it off after the news.

What are you going to do about the phone?

### Haley_Pendragon — 2017-01-15T22:14:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206876>

I've been struggling to find time to read lately between work and kids. I've decided I will try taking the train to work since a new train line went in recently, as I usually have an hour or more drive to work and can use that time much more effectively if I read on the train. I will also get more sun, exercise and fresh air with the 1km walk from the station to work. It will be better for my car too, not being stuck in stop-start motorway traffic.

Problem is, it sounds like a lot of the new carriages have wifi. Hopefully the walk from the station will help to negate that, and I'll cover up as much as possible. My biggest positive is time to read!! Self experiment with 'training it' starts today!

### caroline — 2017-01-16T03:09:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206884>

Hi Haley - great plan. But - I now find I hate taking the train to Sydney - way too much EMF and I don't feel good and very tired. That is probably why the first thing I would do is to by coffee.

How are the kiddos???

### Haley_Pendragon — 2017-01-16T09:21:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206898>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=206884)
>
> Hi Haley - great plan. But - I now find I hate taking the train to Sydney - way too much EMF and I don't feel good and very tired. That is probably why the first thing I would do is to by coffee.
>
> How are the kiddos???

I'll have to see how this train thing goes. My trip in I was in a double seat with all 3 others on their mobile phones while my phone was on airplane mode. My back is also very sore from carrying my backpack so I'm not sure if I will be able to physically keep it up. But on the plus side, I did get lots of reading done and so much sun that my shoulders were slightly burnt!

The kids are good thanks AC, current focus is dealing with a very stubborn 7 yo... She is a super emotional girl, who has always had the attitude of a teenager and seemingly getting worse. Young pendragon is moving ahead in leaps and bounds!

### caroline — 2017-01-17T03:55:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206977>

^^^^^awesome Haley! well, except for your 7yo teenager! How does she sleep? diet? what is she like at her Dad's?

maybe you need a wheelie briefcase .......???

### Haley_Pendragon — 2017-01-17T05:26:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206988>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=206977)
>
> ^^^^^awesome Haley! well, except for your 7yo teenager! How does she sleep? diet? what is she like at her Dad's?
>
> maybe you need a wheelie briefcase .......???

I used to take a wheelie one when I was going to college and also having to take my breast pump, but it was a real problem on a packed train as it doesn't fit between my legs and the seat in front.

With our 7yo, that gets difficult as she spends more time at her dads than mine so I can't control for those factors when she's not with me. He does ok with food, although not up to my standard and she's always had a real issue with fruit and veg, which we have worked with since she was really little - he gives her green powders sometimes I think, and we both persist and make a lot of food with the veggies 'hidden' to overcome that. She is obsessed with sushi and will also sit and smash a whole packet of seaweed if she can! A real savoury kid. Sleep can occasionally be hard, and when she's at mine she will go to sleep with music without too much fuss but if she can't sleep she usually reads for a little while. I think a lot of her issues come down to the large amount of attention she has always gotten from her dad (I went back to work when she was 10 wks old) and now the lack of time she gets with me. We are working on the latter, but her dad has been very difficult about it. She has a very similar attitude at her dads, as far as I'm aware, but he has more time to spend with her one-on-one, and it's the lack of attention or being overtired that really sets her off. It's very draining and I'm often in tears when she leaves on Sunday night as it hurts that she spends a lot of time arguing... Working on it!

### caroline — 2017-01-17T05:55:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-206989>

sending you a hug xo

### Haley_Pendragon — 2017-02-25T00:18:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journey-to-feel-alive.17351/post-209253>

Off to go get another blood test this morning. Just had X-rays on my SIJ, seeing the rheumatologist again next week to try and determine which type of inflammatory arthritis I have (HLAB27 positive)... She's thinking maybe AS due to my SIJ involvement but I'm trying to stay positive! I've already decided a big fat no to the suggested drug of sulfasalazine, horrible stuff.

I got my hands on an acupuncture heat lamp with an infrared bulb a couple of days ago. It's been great for my back, knees and stomach! At the moment I'm doing half an hour a day where I rotate under it, then it dings when my time is up - kind of makes me feel like a roast chook when the timer goes off, haha. Ding, you're cooked!

### Flawed — 2016-01-08T14:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/black-light-directions-of-use.17352/post-185742>

Hey,
Today i tried my black light setup for the first time. A few things i'm not sure about:

How long should i spend under them? (of course that's dependent on amount of am sun i got and skin colour, but approx how long?)

Look directly at them?

Distance from them?

Time of the day they're acceptable to use?

Thanks

### lohd2015 — 2016-01-08T19:03:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/black-light-directions-of-use.17352/post-185774>

> [Flawed said:](/goto/post?id=185742)
>
> Hey,
> Today i tried my black light setup for the first time. A few things i'm not sure about:
>
> How long should i spend under them? (of course that's dependent on amount of am sun i got and skin colour, but approx how long?)
>
> Look directly at them?
>
> Distance from them?
>
> Time of the day they're acceptable to use?
>
> Thanks

I have my black lights on all day in the living room where I do most of my 'work' when there it is a cloudy day outside. At night, before I go to sleep, I generally spend at least 45 mins to one hour under one situated right above my bed (replacement of the ceiling fan light). I find that during the day, it gives me extra energy and at night, it helps me sleep better.
The first time I used it, I looked directly at it for over 10 minutes and it gave me a slight headache. Now I look at it from time to time with no ill effects. I have found it unnecessary to focus on the bulb directly. This is just my personal experience.
Hope it works for you too, Flawed.

### Pebbles — 2016-02-01T18:37:26+0200

# Thread: Apps that can be used to listen to the blogs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/apps-that-can-be-used-to-listen-to-the-blogs.17361/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rubicon — 2016-01-10T18:18:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/apps-that-can-be-used-to-listen-to-the-blogs.17361/post-185911>

Typically I generally prefer listening to audiobooks vs reading text, and I wanted to share a method I've been using to listen to the blogs which involves using the app "Pocket":

1.  Download Pocket to your phone ([Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.ideashower.readitlater.pro&hl=en)) ([iPhone](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/pocket-save-articles-videos/id309601447?mt=8))
2.  Login to Pocket and browse to the blog page you'd like to listen to. On mobile, you should have an option to "Share to Pocket". Alternatively, a method I use is to get the [Pocket Chrome extension](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/save-to-pocket/niloccemoadcdkdjlinkgdfekeahmflj?hl=en) and save the blogs I want to listen do via desktop.
3.  Once you've saved some blog posts to Pocket, open the Pocket app and you'll see a list of saved blogs. You can now put your phone into airplane mode, view the the blogs, and select, "Listen (TTS)"
4.  Pocket will now read the blog to you in a computer voice using Text To Speech (TTS)

In general the computer voice & pronunciation in the app is pretty good. I like to go for a 30-60 min walk as the app reads a blog to me.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Does Amniotic fluid have the best EZ potential in mammilian life?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-amniotic-fluid-have-the-best-ez-potential-in-mammilian-life.17362/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2016-01-10T18:29:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-amniotic-fluid-have-the-best-ez-potential-in-mammilian-life.17362/post-185912>

Woke up for some reason thinking about what might be the best EZ water in the human body, and my first thoughts ended up here. Then it made me think, what happens when the mother is getting a poor water source or is simply dehydrated for a period of time...

Assumption: When dehydration and/or nnEMF rips away a good EZ potential, neurogenesis is going to be compromised resulting in higher probability for autism issues.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15295384](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15295384)

**General stuff on Amniotic Fluid:**

**Contents\[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Amniotic_fluid&action=edit&section=2)\]**
At first, amniotic fluid is mainly water with [electrolytes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrolyte), but by about the 12-14th week the liquid also contains [proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein), [carbohydrates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbohydrate), [lipids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipid) and [phospholipids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phospholipid), and [urea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urea), all of which aid in the growth of the fetus.

**Analysis\[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Amniotic_fluid&action=edit&section=7)\]**
Analysis of amniotic fluid, drawn out of the mother's abdomen in an [amniocentesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amniocentesis) procedure, can reveal many aspects of the baby's genetic health. This is because the fluid also contains fetal cells, which can be examined for genetic defects.

Amniotic fluid normally has a [pH](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PH) of 7.0 to 7.5.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amniotic_fluid#cite_note-Nassos2009-5)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-10T22:10:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-amniotic-fluid-have-the-best-ez-potential-in-mammilian-life.17362/post-185925>

infant life........but not air born life

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-10T22:52:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maintaining-some-hydration-in-a-microwaved-world.17364/post-185932>

When you have no electrons in your protein lipid rafts you lose cell water........microwaves cause dehydration naturally because they vibrate water. This is why when you microwave a left over steak it tastes like shoe leather. The Microwaving Kresser blog covers it.

Why am I optimistic that our species can get back on track with out use of technology?In this video you can clearly see they are copying what a mitochondria does at its two cell membranes and in the matrix where it concentrates hydrogen........one big problem remains for them......they are doing it electrically and chemically ONLY. Mitochondria use those two pathways as well, but they add in a layer of photonics because cytochrome one is a fluorophore protein for UV light. Why does a mitochondria introduce photonics and optics to the full cell of life? It lowers the native electric and magnetic fields to limiting entropy. That is the next step engineers need to consider. When you add light you being to shrink circuits and sizes required to do work. Light allows you to create anything you want because it makes matter programable for a cell. Light transfers energy, information, and momentum data to a cell without being encumbered by mass. Light has no mass. This is why life uses light to power itself. The photo-electric effect can be used to power any electro chemical battery. Life has shown Toyota where to go. When will technology companies realize the same? After all this conversion will save mankind from it current choice of energy generation which create damage via nnEMF and blue light because they only use electricity and magnetism in their circuit boards. Semiconductors of technology are silicon based and life's choices are fabled using light frequencies and amplitudes.Light orders all matter in us and in the universe. All matter is made up of atoms. So how does the science of physics explain this?
Net solar radiation on Earth are a manifestation of the balance between incoming and outgoing shortwave and long wave radiation. All radiations are waves that have amplitude and frequencies. Awakening health within your cells is a synonym for raising one’s frequency while yoking its amplitude to that rise in intensity. As intensity rises you become aware of different perspectives, from which, you begin to resonate with.

These solar radiation are built into ecosystems that directly self organize and are self correcting. This occurs by affecting net radiations primarily through the albedo of the earth and atmosphere (shortwave reflectance or absorption) This is why Tensegrity 7 was and is critical for you to understand. We need sulfated lipids and proteins in our skin to diffuse solar radiations to reflect harmful frequencies and absorb healthy one. Plants do the same by altering the chemistry on their leafs. Humans must have the same ability in their skin and we no longer do. This is why dermatologists believe the sun is bad for us. Solar radiations have to be optimized for water chemistry. Water absorbs best at 270 nm and it is most charge separated by IR and UV (infrared and ultraviolet) light to build a huge battery for living things. Everyone seems to know that electricity runs freely through water. What most do not know is that it becomes more powerful and useful to build complexity when the water is charge separated by solar radiations in the IR and UV range. IR is a synonym for heat.

Heat flux is incredibly important in our quantum ecosystem on Earth. It connects plants to animals cells. When we consider timescales a year or greater, the Earth is in a state of radiative equilibrium, meaning that the Earth releases as much energy as it absorbs. On average, Earth emits 79% of the absorbed solar energy as low energy long wave radiation (3000 to 30,000 nm) due to its relatively low surface temperature. The remaining energy is transferred from Earth’s atmosphere by evaporation of water. This process is called latent heat flux. It accounts for 16% of terrestrial energy losses. The transfer of heat to the air from the warm surface to the cooler overlying atmosphere is called the sensible heat flux. This accounts normally, for 5% of terrestrial energy losses. These statements are how phase transition of energy flows happen between the sun and Earth naturally. This transfer of energy, or phase transition, dramatically affects water cycles on Earth. Heat absorbed from the surface when water evaporates is subsequently released to the atmosphere when water vapor condenses, resulting in the formation of clouds and precipitation. All forms of nnEMF’s (non native EMF) alter the balance of these heat transfers because they cause a dehydration effect in the concentration of water. This is why the donut hole in Australia and New Zealand is one of the main driver’s of illness down under.
The atmosphere also transduces energy flows from the sun to life on Earth below to affect it. How? The atmosphere naturally absorb’s much of the long wave solar radiation. Without this natural effect the surface temperature of the Earth would be 33 degrees celsius low than it is today. Conversely, when those gases rise or when nnEMF fill the atmosphere the temperature can be expected to soar and oceans will acidify very quickly. The atmosphere transmits about half of the incoming shortwave solar radiations to the Earth’s surface. It absorbs 90% of the long wave infrared radiations emitted by the surface. This is how ground water becomes energized by the sun via the atmosphere in coupled fashion. The IR radiated from the Earth techtonic plates fills it with a constant source of IR light. The only thing water needs to become the ultimate battery for life is to be adjacent to any protein and exposed to UV sunlight to grow its exclusion zone. This make water a stronger battery. Plants do this and so do we.Soon our cars might too it appears.

### Aerose91 — 2016-01-14T19:08:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-uv-against-your-skin-safe.17365/post-186282>

@[Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/) @[thomas](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/11019/)

Im grounded 10-12 hours/day and drink close to or including 1 gallon of r.o. water.

I can handle full sun early morning from sunrise (about 6:30) to about 9:30 with little to no clothes. Im also grounded during this time. After that i get dressed and find shade under a tree (still no shoes) where i stretch, meditate, play my guitar- whatever. However, if im feeling ok ill stick a leg or arm out of the shade to get as little bit extra sun as i can.
I can get back into full sun around 3:00 and take my shirt off until sunset around 4:45-ish, still grounded the whole time. The only time im not earthing is when im sleeping on my foam pad or in town to get supplies.

However, this is the weakest point of the year for the sun here so as the months progress and the sun gets stronger & hotter i don't know what ill be able to handle. Eventually I'll have to leave around march/april when the Mohave turns into an oven.

This is where i stay:

# Thread: Brian's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brians-journal.17368/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Bjclifto — 2016-01-11T05:44:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brians-journal.17368/post-185949>

Hey guys,

I started following Dr. Kruse's blog about a year and a half ago. I need to do a lot more homework reading over past blog entries, but just joined as a Gold member to speed up my learning curve by listening to past webinars/QAs. I really enjoy listening to podcasts with Jack so this format of listening to webinars/QAs is highly beneficial in connecting dots with past blogs.

We just got off the January 2016 QA and it was made apparent that I need to step my game up and start a journal. I'm hoping this journal will hold me accountable and provide some additional insights on becoming more optimal for me and my family. Here's some background:

\- Grew up in northern Illinois, healthy as a kid (although we had powerlines roughly 150 meters behind house), spent pretty much all day outside
\- Moved to Germany at age 5 and lived there for three years
\- Moved back to northern Illinois at age 8, still healthy
\- Spent every summer from age 14-19 caddying (morning UV without knowing how good it was for me)
\- Parents are both relatively healthy (mom is pre-diabetic)
\- Never really got sick until during/right after college (found out I was anemic from routine bloodwork)
\- Did more testing and found out I had polyp in small intestine, had it surgically removed at age 23
\- Bloodwork from age 23-27 has been fairly normal (high bun/creatinine ratio), have also had high blood pressure (135/85)...need to be better about tracking these measurements
\- Haven't had bloodwork done in a couple years so definitely need to get on that ASAP
\- Found Dr. Kruse's work through listening to Ben Greenfield podcast
\- Started implementing more DHA, sun, RO water, blue blockers after sunset, CT, and limiting nnEMF

I'm now 30 years old. I've been living in Evanston, IL (basically Chicago) for roughly eight years. My wife and I have a 14 mo old son (born 11 weeks early) and he is one of the main reasons I'm on this journey to become more optimal.

My son was born weighing just 2 lb 13 oz. He spent 99 days in the ISCU. During his stay in the ISCU he had a Grade 1 intraventricular hemorrhage (least severe of brain bleeds), double inguinal hernia surgery, some feeding issues, and was on oxygen for a while.

He's now 14mo and doing well despite his rough start. He's 21 pounds and hitting all of his corrected age milestones. We are lucky that he was able to latch and he's still breastfeeding 4+ times per day. (I've got the wife eating fish at least 3x per week). I still carry a lot of worry/guilt based on his early arrival, but am trying to do everything possible to ensure he has an optimal future. I know from an evolutionary perspective he wasn't supposed to make it. It scares me a ton knowing that, but the best I can do is give him the best environment/light/tools to have a bright future. I'm assuming his preterm birth was due to a huge circadian mismatch/nnEMF/bad light environment. All the doctors could really tell us was that my wife may have had a weakened cervix, but that wasn't determined. Everything in her pregnancy before his abrupt entrance to the world was normal. His preterm birth is one of the primary reasons I'm on this site looking for answers to help him and potentially our future kids (too soon to even think about right now  )

I purchased the Quantlet and am eagerly awaiting it's arrival. This device will be highly beneficial not only from a performance perspective, but more importantly, overall well-being for me and my family from it's other applications which Jack and Rubin have hinted at. My biggest issue is undoubtedly where we live. We live in an apartment where I can pick up 20 wifi signals, we do our best to mitigate these risks, but it's not enough. Despite our current location, I sill try to make sure the whole family gets out a couple times a day to get sun, we all eat seafood, and our little guy is still breastfed.

WE NEED TO MOVE, but I'm getting some pushback from the wife. She's bought in on the blueblockers at night, eating seafood, drinking a lot of RO water, getting sun, turning off wifi when we don't need it, limiting electronics in apartment....but convincing her to move south is difficult. We have a lot of family and friends just south in Indianapolis and at this point that's as far south as she's wanting to go. So hopefully with some additional ammo I can convince her otherwise, especially for our boy....We're headed to Playa del Carmen in late February so maybe this will be a stepping stone in the right direction. That way I can say, "hey, wouldn't it be awesome to have this weather/environment all the time?"

I think that's all for now. I apologize for the randomness of the post. I'm normally in bed now so I probably could use some sleep...did I mention I have a 14mo old

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-11T06:24:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brians-journal.17368/post-185951>

start with vacations directly south from Chi town.........Playa del Carmen, Cancun, Merida, Cozumel etc.........so she can see and sense what it is that she is missing. The weak cervix = faster cervical clock to SCN. Did mom put a laptop in the lap or a cell phone in the pocket? My worry with this story is the transgenerational epigenetics in this case. Makes me want to know more about Mom last ten yrs and her mom and grandmother........will tell you about the kid's mitonuclear coaptation and about the environment they are in..........Chi town is really no bueno du to LED's, nnEMF, and severe population density. The lake and water is solid......so is the cold...........but that is about it. At least youre not on Michigan Ave.
